# **Monday Nite Footballers Commentary**
By Jim Nunnelly

# Doug Williams: Testing His Limits And Succeeding

To dream anything that you want to dream. That is the beauty of the human mind. To do anything that you want to do. That is the strength of the human will. To trust in yourself, to test your limits. That is the courage to succeed.

Those last two lines... "To trust in yourself, to test your limits. That is the courage to succeed." Could very well become the theme for Doug Williams, the Washington Red skin quarterback, as the starting Redskin field gen eral, Doug could become the first black quarterback to lead a pro team to the all-important Super Bowl. While we will see how the theme applies, let's also see how Doug has tested his limits—three times in his life.

First off, his days at Grambling State univer sity. There, Doug had a fruitful and rewarding col lege football career. His "test" rested in the fact that his older brother had played baseball there be fore him and "little brother Doug" was tested and un afraid, but not knowing quite why. Under the steady, guiding hand of Coach Eddie Robinson, starting in 1974, Doug went on to toss 93 touchdown passes and become a viable candidate for the Heisman Trophy four years later. Not bad for a tall, lanky kid from Zachary, La, (near Baton Rouge). Now, watch Doug go on to take a bigger test.

On college draft day that next April, Doug was se lected by the last place, lowly Tampa Bay Bucca neers. Two battles faced him—being a black NFL quarterback and playing for a cellar-doomed team. To the contrary, Tampa Bay went on to participate in three post season playoffs, all under the rifle like arm of Doug Williams.

After a contract dispute with Tampa Bay, Doug left in 1983 to join the now defunct United States Foot ball League.

When Doug had to look for someplace to play, the Washington Redskins signed him away from the "needy and greedy" Los Angeles Raiders. To date, he is a seven-year veteran in the National Football League and has passed for 84 touchdowns (almost equalling his Grambling output). In addition, he has had eleven 300 yard games and single-handedly resurrected the 1987 Red skins by taking over and pushing them into the playoffs. Testing his lim its—all the way! Go ahead, Doug! Now for the third test.

The final time Doug had to test his limits was also in 1983. This time it was not professional—it was personal. Unexpected and unplanned for his college sweetheart and wife, Janice, passed away from a shocking and surprising brain tumor. That sad event left Doug a widower, with a 3-month old daughter, Ashley. Of all the tests faced by Doug, this was the hardest and the most painful test to experience. With every pass of the football and every passing day he re-members her and contin- ually forces himself to an other and different test— another and different limit.

**Commentary**

Obviously, we Monday Niters far too well know the Doug Williams story and we have known it for a long time. But, all this past week, after the Wash ington victory over Chi cago, sportscast and sport caster's have offered Doug's name—some in awe, but all in respect. They are finally realizing his contributions to and greatness in the NFL.

We, on the other hand, look at it a bit differently. In fact, Ike Gardner wond ers what Doug would have been like, if he had good receivers—to the likes of Miami or San Francisco. All of us knew that Doug was great, given half a chance. And. Joe Gibbs, the Washington Redskin coach, was an important play in Doug's hard-earned "game of chance."

Once again, this Sunday, Doug will trust in himself to test his limits. And, if his history continues to repeat itself, he will master the courage to succeed and lead his team to and through the Super Bowl, come January 31.

With and without Janice.

TITLE: Monday Nite Footballers Commentary: Super Bowl Sideline: Past And Present
AUTHOR: Ed West
ENTITIES: Super Bowl, Vince Lombardi, Joe Namath, Len Dawson, Max McGee, Green Bay Packers, Kansas City Chiefs, Washington Redskins, Denver Broncos
---
# Monday Nite Footballers Commentary
By Ed West
Guest Columnist

# Super Bowl Sideline: Past And Present

It was January 15, 1967
and Super Bowl I was about
to begin. The Green Bay
Packers and the Kansas
City Chiefs would battle
for the first championship
crown. The National Foot-
ball League and the Amer-
ican Football League had
been bitter rivals before
both leagues merged to
form today's NFL with two
conferences.

From game one to this
day, some of the most ex-
citing action of Super Bowl
I had occurred off the field
in the days leading up to
the gala event. The night
before Game I, Max McGee
of the Packers violated the
great Vince Lombardi's
curfew to pay his respects
to a blonde he had met
the night before. Since he
hadn't played for six weeks,
he knew there was no
chance he was going to
play unless Boyd Dowler
got hurt. He waddled in
about 7:30 in the morning
and could barely stand up
for the kickoff. On the
bench, the injured Paul
Hornung asked Max what
could he do if he had to
play and Max said, "No
way, there's no way I could

make it."

They were sitting there
discussing Paul's wedding
when Lombardi suddenly
yelled McGee, "get in the
game." Max almost faint-
ed. Dowler was hurt and
McGee played the rest of
the game and caught seven
passes and scored two
touchdowns. After the
game, old Vince came up
to Max and said, "Nice
game." Max replied, "Most
anyone could have done
it" to which Vince ans-
wered, "I know it."

Television rights to the
first Super Bowl game were
awarded to the CBS Net-
work with 365 outlets and
the NBC with 225 outlets.
Estimates of the number
of television viewers
ranged up from 60 million.
One-minute commercials
sold for $75,000 on NBC
and $85,000 on CBS.
Members of the winning
team received $15,000 each
and the losers share was
$7,500 per man.

**Super Bowls II and III**
The only exciting thing
about Super Bowl II was
the rumor circulating that
Lombardi would resign as
coach of the Packers after

the game. Following a 33-
14 victory over the Raiders,
Lombardi did just that.

Super Bowl III was the
"Broadway Joe Show."
Three days before the
game, Joe Namath ap-
peared before the Miami
touchdown club, tossed a
few footballs into the au-
dience, jested briefly and
then, growing serious an-
nounced: "The Jets will
win on Sunday, I guarantee
it." Such a pronouncement
was almost without prece-
dent in American sports,
especially because the
speaker represented a
team that was judged an
18 to 23 point underdog
against the Powerful Bal-
timore Colts. After a 16
to 7 victory, Namath said,
"I'm only talking to the
New York writers. They're
the only ones who believed
me."

**Chiefs In
Superbowl IV**
For Len Dawson, the 1969
season had been a night-
mare. He sat out six games
with a knee injury. His
father died and when the
Chiefs lost their final reg-
ular season game to Oak-
land, a cloud of controversy
swirled about the veteran
quarterback because of a
questionable game plan.
Harassment grew so for-
midable that the Dawson
children were reluctant to

go to school and Mrs. Daw-
son hesitated to mingle so-
cially. Dawson thought that
nothing more could heap
further grief on him and
the Chiefs but on Tuesday
before Super Bowl IV, news
broke that Dawson was im-
plicated in a federal sports
gambling investigation. Af-
ter spending a restless
week, the Chiefs pounded
out a 23 to 7 victory over
the Minnesota Vikings. One
representative of the AFL,
remembering that the
Chiefs had finished second
in the Western Division,
quipped, "Imagine What
one of our first place teams
would have won by."

**Commentary**

From those early begin-
ning to the present, the
sidelines have produced
some exciting stories. The
appearance of the first
black quarterback in the
Super Bowl has to be the
most significant news item
heading into Super Bowl.
Doug Williams will lead
the Washington Redskins
against John Elway and
the Denver Broncos. Based
on Jimmy the Greek's anal-
ysis of the black athlete's
superior breeding, this
should be an exciting game
on the field. Good luck,
Doug. Black America will
be pulling for you.

TITLE: Monday Nite Footballers Commentary
AUTHOR: Jim Nunnelly

# Monday Nite Footballers
**Commentary**
**By Jim Nunnelly**

# From Smith To Bias and Rogers: Where To Next?

Remember Lonnie
Smith. A couple of years
ago, he stated that cocaine
was an everyday “treat!”
for professional athletes.
Easy to get to. Ample supp-
liers. That revelation (the
Lonnie Smith story) set
off a series of sports head-
lines that would cast doubt,
shadow, and shame on our
sports world.

Dozens of pro athletes
made headlines. “Star Play-
er Busted for Cocaine” or
“Star Player fails Drug
Testing.”

My, at times, it appeared
that a new Olympic sport
had been created—coke-
ball. But, thank God it was
all arrests and suspensions.

But in the last month,
Len Bias and Don Rogers
came to pay the ultimate
price for cocaine. These
healthy, undeveloped su-
perstars fell victim to the
fine white powder. Prior
to that, we Monday Niters
had not commented on the
drug problem in sports.
But we can no longer stand
aside and remain silent.
Let’s look at all this and
see what we can say ‘to
clarify the issues. What
are the solutions?

Well, first the drug test
for the athletes.

Without a doubt, Paul
Booker and Jerry Price
support drug testing for
athletes, especially the
pros. Though an invasion
into privacy, the drug test
will at least send a strong

message to young, aspiring
athletes that drugs are not
condoned and will not be
tolerated in the pro leagues.

And Carl Clark goes furth-
er. He says we’d better
start checking the youngs-
ters, too. He reminds us
that every child, seven or
eight, is a potential “coke”
victim. No one, but no one,
should be exempted.

The message to youngs-
ters would appear and re-
sound even stronger. Ob-
viously, Coach Jerry Price
would be pleased for this
approach would not single
out only the athletes, but
would include all students.
Ollie Hubbard would also
support this “early detec-
tion” approach.

Does it sound like we’re
searching for answers?
Well, we are and some go
beyond the classroom. Like
Ed West feels, we should
go after the suppliers, some-
body, somewhere is making
a lot of money preying on
these successful, talented
and rich young athletes.

Where are they? Is this
enough? When and where
do we start? Who should
be first or second? Should
managers, nurses, law-
yers, bookkeepers be drug
tested? What should the
parent’s role be? The
Coach’s role? The prin-
cipal’s role? The commun-
ity’s role?

As you can see, we don’t
know all the answers. We
don’t even know all the

questions. We sure don’t
know where to go next.
We only know that athletes
should be tested and maybe
education ought to start
way down in grade school.
Further, we don’t know
how or when or even if
it will work at all. But let’s
talk more about this timely
subject for a while, ask
more questions and see
what solutions may devel-
op.

**Where to next?**
Members of the Monday
Niters are; Charles Allen,
D.D.S., C. Ashley, Paul
Booker, Tom Brown, Carl
Clarke, Ike Gardner, Ollie
Hubbard, Jim Nunnelly,
Jerry Price and Ed West.

# Monday Nite Footballers
Commentary
By Isaac Gardner Jr.
...................................................

# A Testimony of Courage

Usually this column discloses the views of ten men who rarely agree on anything concerning sports. The ladies of the Monday Night Footballers infrequently get the credit and recognition they fully deserve. The following episode is a testimony of the unquestioned courage of one of the Monday Night Ladies.

Late last Thursday afternoon, Mrs. Janice Nunnelly, known to the group as Grun, received a call from her bank. The caller indicated there was a lady at the bank trying to cash a check, claiming that she was Janice Nunnelly. Grun rushed to the bank, "faster than a speeding bullet." The imposter was being detained by bank officials, still maintaining that she was Janice Nunnelly after Grun brought forth a veritable plethora of identification, clearly establishing her identity. Meanwhile, the imposter feigned thirst, pretended to go to the water fountain, but then fled through the door. Grun, observing the get away, followed the imposter in hot pursuit.

The imposter made it to her car, but Grun, the Swift, was upon her as she started the car. As the car was pulling off, Grun reached through the door and grabbed the imposter. While the car was in motion, Grun, with the grace of a ballet dancer, wrapped her legs around the mirror of the car. The car sped down Prospect with Grun persistently holding on to the neck of the imposter. After a sojourn of seven blocks, Grun finally decided that discretion was the better part of valor and managed to disengage herself from the car and the imposter's neck. Grun escaped with minor scratches.

Grun, now more persistent than ever, searched the neighborhood on foot. With Grun's information, the police apprehended the imposter, who is presently incarcerated.

We constantly hear that members of our community do not want to get involved. The incident described in this article clearly belies that notion as a generality. There may be some who would question Grun's judgement in pursuing the imposter. However, no one would question her courage. The Monday Night Footballers are proud to have her as a member, and I feel honored to be in a privileged few who have her as a personal friend.

# **Monday Nite Footballers Commentary**
# **Brian Tobey: Full of Roots**

**By Jim Nunnelly**
When Alex Haley wrote
*Roots*, he was telling us
that the farther we can
trace our family tree, the
better our chances are be-
coming successful.

And, when the Kansas
City Chiefs drafted Brian
Tobey is round eight on
Monday, they didn't realize
that Tobey had something
extra. Let's see how his
chances for a successful
pro careeer are enhanced
as a result of his many
“roots.”

First, Brian Tobey al-
ways wanted to play pro
football. All of his 23 years
and 250 pounds have been
directed toward this very
practical dream-of one day
playing in the National Foot-
ball League.

According to his father,
Lawrence, Brian did ev-
erything conducive to as-
suring himself a chance
to play-by training hard,
eating well, and choosing
the right “mentor.” That
leads us to the second set
of “roots.”

**Served Under**
**Eddie Robinson**
Brian chose to play col-
lege football at Grambling,
under legendary coach Ed-
die Robinson. With his jun-
ior college All-American
status, Brian could have
gone to Syracuse or Iowa
or even Clemson. But, the
strict rules of conduct, the
simple offer of a challenge,
and a good education, at-
tracted Brian to Louisiana.
And, Brian met the chal-
lenge. Here’s his report
card under Robinson.

Third in the league with
769 yards rushing. Six touch-
downs. Ten receptions. Se-
lected second team All
Southwest Athletic Confer-
ence. Only Lewis Tillmon
of Jackson State, who shat-
tered all of Walter Payton’s
rushing records was ahead
of him. And, Brian achieved
all of this in spite of the
pass-oriented offense at
Grambling.

Grambling’s athletic of-
ficial, Stanley Lewis, went
on to call Brian a “classy
young man”-not only be-
cause of his willingness
to block, but also because
of his outstanding char-
acter and his achieve-
ments.

Let the record show, Bri-
an is hardly a typical black
athlete entering the pro
realm. He has all the family
support he needs.

**Outstanding Heritage**
Brian Tobey is of African
and Indian heritage. And,
his family can trace their
roots, back to Cape Verdan,
West Africa. To go deper,
Brian's family can claim
another distinction, the fam-
ily business.

Opening in 1865, his fam-
ily’s sanitation business
is the oldest, longest-run-
ning, black business in
Hyannis, Massachusetts.
And, maybe, even, in the
entire country. That busi-
ness has been operating
for nine generations and
has always been led by
a Tobey.

And, this very successful
business has withstood all
business fluctuations and
temporary temptations to
sell. Even severe financial
emergencies were some-
how overcome. Employing
21 people, this business
has endured, when many
others have failed. And,
whatever else Brian may
become in the NFL, he is
assured that he has another
option. Sort of a prepared
place. He can always run
the family business. All
because of “these roots.”

**Final Commentary**

So, you can see Brian
Tobey is just “full of roots.”
He comes to the Chiefs,
with a good, playing record,
a comprehensive Robinson-
education, and a solid fam-
ily background. He’s drug-
free, clean-cut, and goal-
oriented. Because of all
these roots, Brian Tobey
will achieve and become
successful in Kansas City.
Look out Palmer and
Okoye!

Though he dosen't have
the publicity or visibility
of a Rodney Peete or a
Derrick Thomas, he will
be a major force in pro
football here. We predict
immediate success.

Alex Haley should meet
Brian Tobey, another
“roots” case history.

**Significant Dialogue**

All of the prophetic Monday Nighters except Carl,
Ollie and "The Commissioner" indicated that DeBerg
would start against San Francisco. Carl and Ollie
indicated that Vlasic was needed to get the Chiefs
out of a pickle. Harry Sypert picked Ty Detmer
to repeat as Heisman Trophy winner. The rest
of the boys who actually know football chose Desmond
Howard of Michigan. The Monday Nighters were
dismayed and disappointed to learn that Harvey
Williams, the Chief's number one draft choice was
seen in the celebrity-frequented Sportsman Barber
shop clad in a Los Angeles Raiders shirt. Hopefully,
as the young man matures he will learn to "dance
with the one that brung you."

# Monday Nite Footballers
Commentary
By Jim Nunnelly

# Lynette Woodard, The First Woman Globetrotter

While it may seem strange that the Monday Nite Footballers would be writing about a basketball player right in the middle of the Super Bowl Playoffs, our interests in athletics and academics is well documented and Lynette Woodard exemplifies one of our key ideals, to assure that athletes also do well in school.

On January 11, the Globetrotters brought their 60-year-old act to Kansas City. Having performed in 10 countries before Kings, Presidents, Shahs and just everyday folks, there was something different this year.

There was Lynette Woodard, the first woman Globetrotter. But there is something real special about Lynette that interests Monday Nite Footballers.

But first, let's look at her career, her performance at Kemper and then we will look at what's "special" about her.

Everyone knows that Lynette Woodard was an All-American at the University of Kansas. There, she scored an amazing 3649 points, surpassing both Wilt Chamberlain and Jo Jo White. She was the captain of the 1984 American Olympic team that won the Gold Medal.

Apparently a diligent community worker, she was honored as NAACP **"Woman of the Year"** by her native city, Wichita, Kas. She has actively been involved in the American Cancer Society and the Big Brothers-Big Sister program. It was 1984 at the Charlotte, N.C. Globetrotter camp, that she achieved history. She was chosen to be the Globetrotters "Sweet Georgia Brown." Affectionately referred to as "Wood," "Little Sister" and "Baby Girl," what court contribution does Ms. Woodard make to the Globetrotters? In Kansas City before a near sellout crowd, she made four layups, blocked one shot, and performed a 6-minute, magic pass routine with teammate—Sweet Lou Dunbar. Not bad for the lightest, shortest, and prettiest Globetrotter. All good but there is a lot more to Lynette.

You see, Ms. Woodard is also a star athlete who has done well in the classroom. Of all her many honors and media attention, seldom is it mentioned that Lynette Woodard was an Academic All American at Kansas University. That means she maintained a B-plus average in college while being a starter in a major sport.

Every young lady and young man in the world should know this fact about Lynette. Unfortunately, most of us will only know that she played at Kemper Arena and she is the attractive star of the Globetrotter television commercial.

We Monday Nite Footballers, honor Lynette Woodard for she has demonstrated that you can do well both on the court and in the classroom. In an exclusive interview with us, she simply gave this advice to young, aspiring athletes. **"Get your education first, and everything else will fall into place."**

It is something to think about.

1. The group recognized Dr. Ada Fisher, Medical Director for Amoco, for her work in worker health care and her gifted speaking ability (Nunnelly).
2. According to a 1990 Kansas City Star Survey, well over half (58%) of the inner city residents stated that a friend or relative had influenced their opinions against drugs (Nunnelly).
3. Derrick Thomas' "Third and Long" program has approached the Monday Niters for possible involvement. More later.
4. Oscar "Baby Brother" Jamerson, one of the original Monday Niters, was in Kansas City over the past weekend.
5. The site of the 1992 Super Bowl is the Metro Dome in Minneapolis (Livingston).
6. Though the Chiefs and the Broncos have the same 7-3 record, Denver has the tiebreaker edge because the Broncos beat the Chiefs earlier in the season.
7. A long fifties discussion about the locations of People's Finance and Twin City Bank (Greenfield, Clark).

# CHATTER, CONVERSATION AND COMMENTARY

1. Last week against Arizona, the Chiefs limited the Cardinals to just 50 rushing yards on 20 tries (2.8 avg.) and sacked QB Dave Kreig seven times while keeping the team out of the end zone all day.

2. The Chiefs are 4-1 to start the year for the second time in three years ( also did it in '93). Kansas City reached the playoffs all six times it started 4-1.

3. The Chiefs defense, which has allowed just seven offensive touchdowns, has gotten outstanding performances so far this year from DT Dan Saleaumua, LB Derrick Thomas and CB James Hasty. Saleaumua has 28 tackles, 22 QB pressures, three sacks, two forced fumbles and one fumble recovery in five games. Thomas, now playing "stack linebacker," is tied for third in the NFL with five sacks and is second on the team with 32 tackles. Hasty, who was named the AFC Defensive Player of the month for September, has had a hand in four of the Chiefs 11 takeaways.

4. COMBAT funds 31 drug prevention and 28 drug treatment agencies across Jackson County. If you or someone you know needs these services, please call the information line at 84-COMBAT or Jim Nunnelly at 881-3814.

6. For the Chargers, punishing RBNatrone Means is the AFC's leading rusher with 504 yards and three touchdowns on 109 carries.

7. Martin Bayless, now with the Chiefs, was the Chargers' starting strong safety from '87-'91. During that time, Bayless collected 355 tackles, three interceptions, 7 1/2 sacks and five fumble recoveries in 73 games in 63 starts). He had a great game against Arizona.

**CHATTER AND CONVERSATION**

1. The head groundskeeper at Arrowhead Stadium is Kansas City native Andrew Bruce. Bruce, a Central High school graduate, was elevated to that position in March, 1990. The NFL Players Association voted Arrowhead Stadium's grass playing surface as one of the best in the League after its installation in 1994.
2. The last time the Chiefs played the Steelers was January 8, 1994, at Arrowhead Stadium, (AFC first round playoff game). It was the second playoff game in Arrowhead Stadium history. The Chiefs upended the Steelers 27-24 to set up a Divisional playoff battle against the Houston Oilers.
3. The defending AFC Champion Pittsburgh Steelers opened the season with a 24-9 loss at Jacksonville but have since rebounded. They have won three straight games over Baltimore, Buffalo and Houston. The core to the success of the Steelers is their defense which is allowing just 281.8 yards per game, fourth fewest in the NFL.
4. Sixteen different Chiefs have caught passes, including 10 with at least 5 catches. Wide Receiver Chris Penn leads the group with 13 receptions for 139 yards and two touchdowns.
5. Running back Marcus Allen is 13th in the AFC with 194 rushing yards and three touchdowns on 56 carries. Linebacker Derrick Thomas is second in the AFC and tied for third in the NFL with six sacks.
6. The Chiefs, up until the 1993 season, had Pittsburgh Steelers Head Coach Bill Cowher as the defensive coordinator. Coach Cowher was in Kansas City for two years (1989-91), under Coach Marty Schottenheimer. Both Derrick Thomas and Neil Smith played under Cowher.
7. COMBAT drug treatment providers are required to take classes on cultural diversity as part of their contractual responsibilities. These classes aid the counselors in meeting the treatment needs of Jackson County’s diverse population.
8. Kordell Stewart is listed as the number three quarterback behind starter Mike Tomczak (18) and backup Jim Miller (16). Stewart is a second-year player from Colorado. So far this season, he has 40 yards rushing and 135 yards receiving with one touchdown. His nickname is “SLASH” because he splits his time as a quarterback/wide receiver (QB/WR). He will be the star on Monday night.

# Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

We'll all be home for New Year's. That's the cry for our Kansas City Chiefs, once thought to be the AFC's entry to the Super Bowl next month. Well, it didn't happen and the once 13-3 Chiefs are now 6-9, and may even go to 6-10, i.e. if Oakland overtakes the Chiefs Saturday.

What happened, you may ask, especially in the last half of the year. The only thing that did not happen negative to the Chiefs was there was no drug-related incidents. And, we who are associated with combating illegal substances are eternally grateful for that. But, there were many other football aspects to ponder and talk about. Here are a few:

*   First, the Chiefs wavered every week about who the starting quarterback would be. That took away from the stability every winning team must have.
*   Cornerback Mark McMillian, size notwithstanding, became every passers' target. His 5'7", 145-pound stature invited opposing quarterbacks to pick on him. They did again and again. We never fixed this glaring gap.
*   Until Bam Morris, we never established a running back. Donnell Bennett was chosen and that was it. The last time you heard Kansas City mentioned as the home of a first-class running back was way back in 1967. Remember Mike Garrett. No running, no playoffs.
*   Kansas City literally set Derrick Thomas up for failure, by making him a "falcon" linebacker (a down lineman). Because of his small size, big 300-pound linemen just tossed him around. Speed is good but size is still a major factor in sports. Derrick made All-Pro as a linebacker, utilizing the quarterback. We ruined his effectiveness.

Then, too, there were other things off the field that distracted the Chiefs as well. I'm not sure how much all of this affected the play. I just know since the October 4 rainy win over Seattle, the Chiefs have spiraled downward and outward. Whatever the reasons, we all will be watching and pulling for Minnesota, Denver, Miami, San Francisco and Atlanta. The Chiefs will take their place as one of the 18 NFL teams whose season will end this week.

For the rest, the second season starts in January.

1. The Chiefs have gained more than 300 total yards in just two of the last nine games against the Raiders, while Los Angeles has not registered more than 280 yards in the past five games vs. Kansas City.
2. Art Shell was named the ninth head coach in Raiders history on Oct. 3, 1989. The 47-year-old Shell had been the Raiders offensive line coach for seven years. He first joined the staff in '83 after 15 seasons as one of the finest offensive tackles in NFL history. An eight time Pro Bowl selection, he was inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame on Aug. 5 of '89. His regular career record stands at 49-38 (.883).
3. On Oct. 3, 1993 at Arrowhead (Game 4), the Chiefs opened up a 21-3 half-time lead on the Raiders, and never looked back in a 24-9 victory.
4. The midway point of the season finds the Chiefs with a 5-3 record for the third time in five years ('90 and '91). The Chiefs opened the season with three straight victories, but have lost three of their last five games.
5. The Los Angeles Raiders have been victorious in three of their last four outings, beating New England (21-17); Atlanta (30-17) and Houston (17-14) while losing in overtime to Miami (20-17). The team's offensive leader is WR Tim Brown who has caught 44 passes for 658 yards with six TDs.

**Monday Nite Footballers Predictions**
By Jim "Grand Dad" Nunnelly
Predicting Since 1970

**Kansas City Chiefs vs. Cincinnati Bengals**
**12:00 p.m. KCTV 5**

| Predictor | Pick | Comment |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Charles Allen (9) | Chiefs by 3 | Chiefs will take a bite out of Bengals |
| Paul Booker (5) | Bengals by 14 | Chiefs not ready for the big show! |
| Carl Clark (8) | Chiefs by 7 | Chiefs not ready for the fat lady to sing! |
| Bubba Cleaver (8) | Unavailable | Unavailable |
| Ike Gardner (8) | Bengals by 8 | Forget season, focus on next year's draft |
| Lloyd Greenfield (9) | Chiefs by 3 | All is well that ends well |
| Ollie Hubbard (3) | Bengals by 6 | Cincinnati has improved, in every way |
| Jim Nunnelly (9) | Chiefs by 10 | Johnson, Kennison bash Bengals |
| Ed West (5) | Chiefs by 7 | Game is meaningless to Cincinnati |
| Eric Wesson (5) | Chiefs by 1 | Bipolar Chiefs come through, hopefully |

**"Forks in the Road"**

Obviously, this game is it.! The Chiefs have come to the fork in the road...either they win or we will all turn our attention and support to Tony Dungy (Indianapolis) and Marvin Lewis (Cincinnati). But if Detroit should happen to beat Pittsburg and Denver wipes out San Diego and the Chiefs beat up on Cincinnati.... then the Chiefs will make the playoffs. Folks, it is that simple. And complicating matters is the hovering speculation that Vermeil will retire ... one way or the other. Clearly, Vermeil is at the fork in the road, too. Win and stay in! Lose and sign up for Medicare! Folks, it is that simple.

And we, Monday Niters, are facing another kind fork in the road. Monday Night Football, as we know it, is over. ABC will no longer broadcast the perennial ratings-winner. In fact, we all said our goodbyes to Al Michaels and John Madden and remi-nisced about all the history-making plays witnessed on Monday night television, everywhere, In 1970, we were all younger, quicker, and immature. Now we are older, slower and fall asleep with out warning. It takes us longer to do EVERY-THING; including selecting our teams. Some of us even have to ask "who's play-ing". The fork in the road for us is that we are just getting old and things are chang-ing, with or without our support. But some things will never change....Ike, Ollie and that Booker will always be haters of the Chiefs. Cleaver, Carl, and Ed West will go with the wind. And faithful, fine people like Lloyd Greenfield and imminent Dr. Allen, will always support the Chiefs, just like on New Year's Day against North Division champions, Cincinnati. We know the Chiefs will win by at least ten and Vermeil stays in. We never have been at that fork in the road!

Like each of you, we will have to switch the channel over to ESPN and carry on as usual. Booker, Ollie and Ike will continue to hate the Chiefs. And Ed, Carl and Cleaver will switch up, every now and then. And Dr. Charles Allen, Lloyd Green-field and I will always support the Chiefs. Wisely, we don't have to face a fork in the road. We know the way, just like we have always done. And just for the record, we will be looking for the Chiefs to win on New Year's Day, out at Arrowhead. Chiefs by 10 and Vermeil will be back, again!

# Monday Nite Footballers Predictions
By Jim "Grand Dad" Nunnelly
Predicting Since 1970

# Kansas City Chiefs
# At
# New York Jets
# 12:00 p.m. KCTV 5

# Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

Let's take a glimpse of what's been happening over the past two weeks in sports. Everybody knows that Michael Jordan is back in the NBA. Wonder how much they are really paying "Medicare Michael" to put his 38-year-old body back out there on the hardwood. And we all have been crying good tears for the Mighty Yankees were shut down. And by the unexpected, youthful Arizona Diamondbacks. And Arizona did it to the Yankees just like the Yankees did it to them . . . in the bottom of the ninth inning. And what about those University of Missouri Tigers? Kareem Rush is on the front cover of Street and Smith magazine. And the national coaches' poll ranks the Tigers eighth, behind fifth-ranked Kansas. And there is only one senior on this year's squad . . . that's Clarence Gilbert. He and Rush will team up with Paulding, Johnson, Gage, and some new freshmen. Perhaps this just might be the year that Mizzou goes forth.

Speaking of going forth, how about those Chiefs, last Sunday, against the San Diego Chargers! Ike and his football Taliban predicted that San Diego would just beat our Chiefs to a pulp. Didn't happen. And, while the Chiefs almost gave the game away, Kansas City even held on in the waning moments, to preserve the victory. It was not like in weeks past, when ol' Spent Trent would throw an interception. Or there wouldn't be enough time outs. Or the special teams would allow a big touchdown to turn the game around. It was as if the Chiefs were managing the passing and running game, effectively.

That word, running. You will remember that Priest Holmes has been showing out lately. In fact, against San Diego's high-ranked run defense, he skipped, dipped, and ripped through Junior Seau and his friends as if he had called "psychic hotline" on them. Scampering 181 rushing yards, Priestly Priest has helped us restore faith in the running game. "Trent Bono" can't pass. But Priest Holmes can run. That is the same thing Marty Schottenheimer has been telling us. I can see ol' Marty standing over there on the sidelines, calling repeatedly for the run. Maybe his "ghost" was still there.

And that's just what apparition means. It means a ghost or an unexpected appear-ance. With Marty now winning three straight at Washington, we should be glad to have ol' Marty around . . . even if it's just his apparition. He apparently know what's best. A running game in hand is worth a St. Louis-style passing game in the bush.

# Monday Nite Footballers Predictions
By Jim Nunnelly

**Green Bay Packers, NFC Champions**
**vs.**
**Denver Broncos, AFC Champions**

Qualcomm Stadium, San Diego, Cal.
Sunday, Jan. 25, At 5 P.M.
KSHB-TV, Channel 41

| MEMBER | PREDICTION | COMMENT |
|---|---|---|
| Charles Allen | Packers by 7 | Continued NFC dominance! |
| Paul Booker | Denver by 6 | Better team! |
| Carl Clark | Packers by 10 | Supporting cast superior! |
| Ike Gardner | Packers by 10 | NFC's defense is better than AFC's offense. |
| Lloyd Greenfield | Denver by 6 | It's Elway turn! |
| Ollie Hubbard | Denver by 10 | Change is gonna come! |
| Walt Livingston | Denver by 20 | Lucky! |
| Jim Nunnelly | Denver by 3 | John Elway, MVP! |
| Harry Sypert | Denver by 3 | Mile High satellite! |
| Ed West | Packers by 14 | Turnovers, turnovers! |

**John Elway: Take A Bow On Super Bowl Sunday**

John Elway is after all used to being on center stage and at the center of attention. That's understandable. He was the first athlete picked in the 1983 draft. He passed up a chance to play baseball for the Yankees. For starters he's brought Denver back to 40 some victories after being behind in the fourth quarter. All that and he's never won a Super Bowl and time is running out on the 15-year All-Pro quarterback.

Before we review Elway's chances for victory in Sunday's Super Bowl game against Green Bay, let's look back and see how "other" quarterbacks have upstaged Elway in past Super Bowl games.

First off, there was Phil Simms at Super Bowl XXI... when the New York Giants quarterback completed 22 of 25 passes that afternoon in Pasadena. Then in Super Bowl XXIV in 1990, Joe Montana passed five touchdowns, helping the 49ers to a 55-10 trouncing of the Broncos. There's more.

But, it was the 1988 Super Bowl that "Big John" will vividly remember. That was "Doug Day afternoon." Quarterback Doug Williams will put on a record setting passing clinic for Elway and African Americans everywhere. Being the first black quarterback to start a Super Bowl, Williams threw four touchdown passes in the second quarter, one for 80 yards to Ricky Sanders. And when it was all over, the Washington Redskins had dwarfed Elway and the Broncos 42-10. Grambling's favorite son and now coach was selected the game's Most Valuable Player, again upstaging one-way Elway. One more time.

But, like many of his comeback victories, Elway has another chance this Sunday. In fact, Ollie Hubbard, Paul Booker, and hate nable Harry Sypert believe it's Denver's time this year. Some others are just loyal to the American Football Conference, who hasn't produced a winning Super Bowl since Marcus Allen starred for the Los Angeles Raiders, way back in Super Bowl XVIII.

Will John Elway once again be the "other" quarterback, taking a back seat to Green Bay's Brett Favre? Carl Clark puts it best when he says Elway has a "cast of supporting characters" and that makes Elway a star and a winner this time.

In years past, he didn't have a Shannon Sharpe, or a running back like Terrell Davis, who on any given day can equal Barry Sanders. And, most of all, he didn't have a young, big-play, wide receiver like Missouri Southerner's Rod Smith. Mix that in with defensive giant Neil Smith and Keith Traylor and you will see clearly why John Elway will get the last bow on Sunday. San Diego will not be able to hold all the hoopla when the Broncos package the Packers. And Dear John will be on the shoulders of his team mates. The TV cameras will be all in his face. He would have done it, won the "big one." And he'll take a bow, right out in the middle of field, where 134 million people will clap and stand up.

Take a bow, John. This Bud's for you.

**Sunday's Super Bowl Tidbits**

* Doug Williams, record-setting Most Valuable Player of Super Bowl XXII, will be part of the opening, coin-toss ceremonies. Williams was the first African American quarterback to start a Super Bowl. Now head coach at Grambling where he played college football, Williams will share pre-game honors with his old coach of the Washington Redskins, Joe Gibbs.

* 134 million people will watch Sunday's Denver-Green Bay Super Bowl. The game will start at 5:12 p.m. and can be seen on Channel 41 (NBC network).

* Lots of creative commercials will be launched on Sunday. Advertisers are paying 1.3 million dollars for each 30 second spot.

* Ralph Brown, owner and operator of the Sportsmen's House of Coiffures, will attend the Super Bowl in San Diego this Sunday. He has been to every Super Bowl but one since the Chiefs in 1970.

# Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim “Grand Dad” Nunnelly

# Super Bowl XXXIV

**Tennessee Titans**
vs.
**St. Louis Rams**

Georgia Dome In Atlanta, 5:18 P.M., Sunday

KMBC-TV, Channel 9

**Chatter, Conversation And Commentary**

Being Super Bowl week, these last few days surely have been eventful. Not only did we get some sad news about Derrick Thomas, we have not fully recovered from the last minute, last quarter incomplete pass call against our beloved Tampa Bay Bucs. That was in the championship game at St. Louis, and we are left wondering why there was even an instant replay review in the first place. That one call erased Tony Dungy's chances of taking his team to the Super Bowl this Sunday in Atlanta. Really, it should be Tampa Bay vs. Tennessee rather than St. Louis vs. Tennessee.

Any way, let's look at the lucky St. Louis Rams. For us in Kansas City, this will be as close to a hometown as we will get. However, because of reasons we'll explain later, most of us will not be pulling for the Rams. By now, we all know just how talent-loaded this "arch-city" team really is. Even if one forgets about Kurt Warner and his 40-plus touchdown passes this season, you still have to contend with wide receiver Isaac Bruce. As if that's not enough, one has to also consider the venerable Marshall Faulk, formerly of the Indianapolis Colts. Now, there's a difference maker...solid runner, great pass receiver and steady four quarters. While the Rams are generally known for their overpowering offense, they are just as respectable on defense. Thus, they will be task-ready for the Tennessee Titans. Let's now turn to the team, formerly known as the Houston Oilers.

Certainly not as powerful on offense as St. Louis, the Titans do have a franchise player named Steve “Air” McNair. A rifle-armed passer, he was good at Alcorn A.M.&N, and he's been great in the National Football League. Paul Booker is betting on him to win the game, just like ol' Doug Williams did back in the eighties Super Bowl. On that day, MVP Doug Williams did everything for Washington. He completed so many passes that the records still stand. That includes John Elway's last stand two years ago. Thus, Doug and Steve McNair are the reasons why most African Americans nationwide will be rooting for Tennessee on Sunday. It will be about ethnic pride that comes with playing in a championship game, with the eyes of the entire world watching you perform.

For us, Tennessee is the best team that has worked the hardest and overcome the most adversity to make it to the top spot. Not just the politics of playing an African American quarterback, but in moving a struggling franchise in a big city (Houston) to a thriving powerhouse in a small city (Nashville). And taking a young team and wielding it into a contender in a very short time. You see, all of this mirrors the migratory struggles that so many of us have faced and thus, we can identify largely with Tennessee.

So, at 5 p.m. Sunday in Atlanta, look for the Titans to be on top all the way.
Here's what we think:

# Monday Nite Footballers Predictions
By Jim "Grand Dad" Nunnelly
Predicting Since 1970

**Pittsburgh Steelers**

**At**

**Kansas City Chiefs**

**12 Noon KCTV 5**

**IKE GARDNER**

**Quote Of The Week**
*"I've read in-depth a good book where man walked on water. But, his name was not Dick Vermeil."*

# Chatter, Conversation And Commentary
You all don't know Robert Lee Allen. He's 7 years old and he attends elementary school out in Martin City. Young, gifted and black, young Allen sums it up quite adequately when he says, "The Chiefs are good. . . sometimes." From the mouth of babes flows the truth.

Fresh from a surprising victory over the lowly, winless Washington Redskins the week before, our Chiefs fell hard to Denver in their new stadium. Denver ran through our run defense as if they had stolen the Chiefs' playbook. And Trent Green threw well, except, he threw it four times to Denver's record-setting cornerback. And, Priest Holmes, was lost without the blocking of Tony Richardson. All in all, the Chiefs made young Allen sound awfully good because their overall performance against Denver, was at best, "sometimey." What adjustments need to be made against the Pittsburgh Steelers?

Well, first off, the run defense needs to do double time. That's because Jerome Bettis, fresh off of attaining 10,000 yards, will test them to the max. Secondly, Trent and his receivers must spend time, just throwing and catching the ball. There is a severe loss of fundamentals in the passing game. And, it has to be fixed before the Chiefs can win another game. Thirdly, the special teams smell. Like the offense, there is something missing. . . blocking, running, just plain aggressiveness. And all of this must be in place before Sunday. That is, if we are to become 2-3.

A record of 2-3 or 1-4, there is not much difference, especially if we don't fix the aforementioned problems. They won't go away. And young Allen will be watching and wondering if Sunday will be the day when the Chiefs get on the win side of "sometimey."

We ought to pay attention to this 7-year-old. So should Dick Vermeil.

**Quote Of The Week**
"With a little optimism, new coach and cast, the rehearsal is over and now the performance begins."
-- Charles Allen

# Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

This Monday night, we, the Monday Nite Football group, will gather at Ed West's house to be in our 32nd year as the city's only official Chiefs' Fan Club. Same guys watching football and conversing for more than three decades. As always, there will be food galore, prepared for members of the larges repository of football history, anywhere in Kansas City. Ike, Carl, Ed and Charles, have had season tickets ever since the early Chiefs were over at the 22nd and Brooklyn site. All will be happier because of the reunion, and as has been the group's policy, no one will leave mad. . . just a bit more enlightened and a lot more prepared. . . to talk anytime, anywhere about their favorite subject. . . the Kansas City Chiefs. Welcome back to our world.

(Image of a young boy)
**ROBERT LEE ALLEN**

# Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

I’ve been talking to Robert Lee Allen. You will remember him because he is the very bright, 7-year-old grandson of Charles and Beverly Allen. And, occasionally, he attends our Monday Nite football meetings. Robert seems to think the Chiefs are really a good team, even though they have not been winning. At first, this seems like a paradox. . . good, but not winning. Let’s explore this wisdom, coming from this talented, Martin City student.

First, it is true that Chiefs have some talented players. They don’t come any better than Tony Gonzales. Robert agrees. And Priest Holmes is showing signs of becoming the next Barry Sanders. Robert agrees with that, too. And, no one is quite sure why Vermeil keeps talking about the mediocre play of the receivers. For the most part, they are ok . . . at least most of the time. So, what’s wrong? Why aren’t these Chiefs winning?

It’s really very simple. It’s not the defense, even though they are not playing optimal-ly. Not the special teams, even though they played only one good game (Washington). Well, what is it? It is simply Vermeil’s refusal to admit that “Bent-Trent” IS the prob-lem. The man leads the NFL in interceptions; has lost four games in the final minutes; and will invariably hold on to the football as if he is trying to get sacked. God only knows why Vermeil holds on to what Ike calls the team’s “weakest link.” But, that’s why Robert Lee Allen is saying that the Chiefs are a good team . . . without Trent. Will we win?

Definitely. Those Chiefs are going to win Sunday. Not because Trent will play bet-ter. And, like another youngster told me . . . you can’t sell tickets to a circus that’s got sick elephants. Number 10 is one of ‘em. From the mouth of babes!!!

# **Chatter, Conversation And Commentary**

There are two important similarities to the two teams in the 2003 Super Bowl. First, they both proudly wave Pirates' flags, one calling themselves the Buccaneers and the other known respectfully as the Raiders. Then, too, each share the influence of a common head coach, Jon Gruden. There, the similarities cease. On the other hand, the Raiders sport the NFL’s Most Valuable Player in quarterback, Rich Gannon. On the other hand, the Bucs have Warren Sapp, arguably the best defensive lineman to ever play the game in recent times. The Raiders will place their receiving hopes in their Medicare acquisition, Jerry Rice. And the Bucs will put their trust in Deion-like, Keyshawn Johnson. All of this makes for a competitive and entertaining Super Bowl, assuring that each team has a reasonable chance to win. Of course, most Monday Niters believe in Oakland. That's because they are 'Raiders worshipers,' never mind go, of the fact that the Chiefs let this rugged individualist star get away. Plus, they feel some faulty pride in that the AFC West produced the most competitive division in football. Nevertheless, the Bucs have a coaching genius in Jon Gruden, and it is my belief that this "coaching factor" is a real key. While some may rightfully argue that the coach will not wear a uniform, leaving player performance as the most important outcome factor on Sunday. But, any respectable football fan, knows that in close games, the coaching effect reigns supreme. Things like clock management, blitz-timing, and playcalling are all decisions directly influenced by the head coaches. So, given that this will be one of the closest Super Bowl games, Jon Gruden becomes the "difference maker" out in San Diego, giving the Bucs the vital edge and their first championship ever.

# Chatter, Commentary and Conversation

In order to obtain relief from pressure, individuals and organizations sometimes do real badly at first. Then, when they improve slightly, everyone thinks of the improvement and forgets the horrible start. Sound familiar? I never thought of Herm Edwards would dip that low.

Whatever the rationale, Of Herm finds himself trying to explain why the Chiefs are off to another bad start. I can tell you that Chiefs fans are not going to take another "dumbing-down" ruse (trickery).

Face it, y'all. The Chiefs looked like a "rag-tag" team out there against Pittsburgh. From start to finish and all points in between, the Chiefs just played to the lowest level ever. The corners didn't cover. The defensive line didn't tackle. The running backs didn't run. And the offensive line didn't block. The only positive thing that emerged last Sunday was the punter. That boy, "Croquette" boomed that ball 70-plus yards. The only bright spot on an otherwise horrible display of Kansas City football. But let's not get sentimental.

Without regard to Herm’s motivational explanation, it still boils down to the Chiefs not playing. Period. Now, he can play that "dumbing-down" card, if he wants. But we old fans know that game. The message is simple: Win against San Diego Sunday or get out of the way!"

Personally, we loyalists are pulling for them. Don't tell the haters.

# Conversation, Chatter And Commentary

**ABOUT THE CHIEFS** -- In the last seven games, Kansas City has sacked the quarterback 33 times and forced 13 turnovers. Still, the Chiefs defense has not yielded a touchdown in the second half for 10 consecutive games. The Chiefs played the Saints last in 1994 and beat them 30-17.

**ABOUT THE NEW ORLEANS SAINTS** -- The Saints have been victorious in four of their last six games, beating Oakland, Seattle, Carolina and Arizona. The Saints will start their fourth quarterback, Billy Joe Hobert.

**GANNON OR GRBAC?** -- The moment of truth is here. Do you dance with one that brung ya? What if they both dance and have brought you forward? Grbac, before he was injured, won seven games, losing only to Denver and Miami. Gannon took over in November and won five games, losing only to Jacksonville. Neither is great, but with a defense full of stars, they are operating well above mediocre. And, we are the AFC West Champions...with two able quarterbacks.

Point is, what do you do when you have to make a choice between two healthy quarterbacks. We discussed this whole thing at length last Monday over at Paul Booker's house. After we feasted, first on Carol's cornbread and her famous friend chicken wings, the following thoughts on the subject emerged:

*   Schottenheimer is hard headed, he won't do the right thing.
*   Grbac needs to be brought along gently.
*   If it ain't broke, don't fix it.
*   The rhythm of the team is with Gannon.
*   Gannon is a backup and now must take a back seat.

Plain and simple the Monday Niters think that Rick Gannon should start. He's the leader; he's got the team in rhythm, and he's won against tough teams like San Francisco and Denver. He's done all the right things. BUT...Marty will probably return the job to Elvis Grbac. Carl Clark says backup quarterbacks are still backup quarterbacks. Don't look for Marty to elevate Gannon. Once a backup quarterback, always a backup quarterback.

# CHATTER, CONVERSATION AND COMMENTARY

DESMOND HOWARD, former Green Bay kick returner, is now an Oakland Raider. His high school and college teammate was Chiefs quarterback Elvis Grbac. After a stellar season at Michigan, Howard went on to become the 1991 Heisman Trophy winner, as a wide receiver. Grbac remains untested and untried.

KEVIN LOCKETT first-year wide receiver for the Chiefs, will be celebrating his 23rd birthday on Monday. Kevin attended Kansas State and is from Tulsa.

LAST WEEK, Oakland lost in overtime to Tennessee (Houston) Oilers. The Chiefs last week was outclassed by Denver, 19-3. Last year, Oakland won seven and lost nine. The Chiefs won nine and lost seven last year. Ironically, the Chiefs beat Oakland at Arrowhead last year, 19-3.

GREG HILL ran for 92 yards last week against Denver...on just seven carries. Every time Hill would run for long yardage, the coaches would take him out. Those kind of bonehead moves has brought on playcalling criticisms of the Chiefs offensive coordinator, Paul Hackett. Look for Greg Hill to rush for 150 yards against Oakland Monday night. He will be the difference in the game.

DONNIE EDWARDS, Chiefs leading tackler with twelve, is a native of Chula Vista, Cal., a suburb of San Diego. Edwards. who won the starting linebacker job from veteran Tracy Semien, also has forced a fumble.

# Monday Nite Footballers And Their Predictions

The Kansas City Chiefs have their second and last NFL bye this week-end and for the Monday Niters, its another week off from their predictions. It's also an opportunity to update their success and quite surprisingly, there is a log jam at the top with a three-way tie including Tom Brown, Carl Clark and Jim Nunnely, each compiling a 4-1 record and 26 points. Also with a 4-1 mark are Lloyd Greenfield (27 pts) and Charles Allen (34 pts). Theirs compare to the Chiefs 5-1 record. The Monday Niters did not begin their season until the second game.

The 1993 campaign looks like it could be quite topsy-turvy whereby the predictors on the bottom could trade places with the leaders on just one pick.

**By Jim Nunnelly**

Kansas City Chiefs vs. Buffalo Bills
Rich Stadium, Orchard Park, N.Y.
12 Noon

TV - Channel 4
Radio - Fox 101.1 FM

| Ollie Hubbard | Buffalo by 8 | Kelly, Kelly, Kelly |
| Jim Nunnelly | Chiefs by 2 | Davis, Smith, Montana |

## Chatter, Conversation & Commentary

1. Marv Levy is in his 8th season as the Bills head coach. The 66-year-old Levy led the Bills to their 4th consecutive AFC championship in 1993. He has never won a Super Bowl. He previously served as head coach of the Chiefs from 1978 to 1992, producing a 31-42 record. He was a collegiate running back at Coe College from 1949 to 1950 and has earned a Master's degree in English History from Harvard. His regular season career record stands at 114-84 (.576).

2. Last week Chiefs RB Marcus Allen dashed 36 yards for a score, his longest TD scamper since 1985. Then he added two more points with a conversion run, putting the Chiefs up, 21-7 over Seattle.

3. The Chiefs are off to their second straight 5-2 start. Last year the club extended that to 7-2. It is hard to believe that the offense the Chiefs have put on the field in wins over Denver and Seattle is the same one that sputtered in losses to the LA Rams and San Diego.

4. Carl Clark, one of the founding members of the Monday Nite Footballers, just recently finished a world tour that included Indonesia, Hong Kong, Singapore, Malaysia and Taiwan. He reported to the group about his experiences in several foreign countries.

6. The solid defensive unit is led by S Eric Turner, who is tied for the NFL interception lead with six. Kicker Matt Stover has made 12 straight field goals and is 18 of 20 for the season.
LB Derrick Thomas had a career-high 13 tackles as a rookie vs. Cleveland on 11/19/89. TE Derrick Walker, starting in place of injured TE Keith Cash, already has 24 catches this year.

# Arrowhead

**Chatter, Conversation And Commentary**

1. The Chiefs acquired Joe Montana from San Francisco last April. In that deal, the Chiefs also received starting Safety David Whitmore and a third round pick (wide receiver Lake Dawson). For the record, Joe Montana was San Francisco's third-round draft choice from Notre Dame and the 82nd player picked in the 1979 draft.

2. In 1991, San Francisco beat the Chiefs at Candlestick Park 28-14. The starting quarterback for the Forty Niners was Steve Bono, now back-up signal caller for the Chiefs.

3. Studies show that children who do drugs and alcohol are twice as likely to start fights and three times at likely to skip school. More information is available at the Jackson County Prosecutor's office. Call COMBAT 881-3555.

4. The Chiefs have opened the season with a victory for five straight years.

5. Jerry Lee Rice, new record holder for most touchdowns in a career, will be 32 on Oct. 13. He was a 1985 first round choice by San Francisco and Bill Ingram in the United States Football League. From Starkville, he starred at Mississippi Valley State. Last time he faced the Chiefs in 1991, he caught seven passes and scored two touchdowns. Rice caught touchdown No. 127 on a 38-yard reception from Steve Young with 4:05 left in the game Monday Night against the Raiders at Candlestick. Rice beat the coverage by former Chief Albert Lewis. Rice scored a total of three touchdowns in the game, including a 69-yard reception from Young in the first quarter and a 23-yard reverse in the fourth quarter. Rice surpassed NFL great Jim Brown of Cleveland who had held the record for touchdowns with 126 for many years.

# Monday Nite Footballer Commentary
By Jim Nunnelly

# A Look At Predicted Starters

The Monday Niters are gearing up for the upcoming National Football season.

Look for our first predictions on the Kansas City Chiefs in the Sept. 2 edition of **THE CALL**.

This week, the Monday Niters take a look at two Chiefs players -- rookie running back **Greg Hill** and veteran safety **David Whitmore**.

**Greg Hill** was the Chiefs first round draft choice and is running the ball well for the Chiefs' offense. He's good in the open field and has stiff-arm strength. He has 4.4 speed and was an honorable mention All-American from Texas A & M. He is expected to play a lot this season.

**David Whitmore** is a talented safety and already is the leading tackler in preseason. he came to the Chiefs as part of the Joe Montana trade with San Francisco. He can intercept, recover fumbles and tackle all. He is expected to be a starter this season.

**GREG HILL**

**DAVID WHITMORE**

**Chatter, Conversation and Commentary**

1. The Kansas City Chiefs 8-3 record would be enough to lead all the NFL divisions except the AFC West. The Green Bay Packers have lost their last two games -- Kansas City and Dallas 18-6, making their record 8-3 also.
2. Cigarettes contain tobacco, which is a drug. Exercising and participating in sports is nearly impossible if you smoke cigarettes. Also, youth who smoke cigarettes are much more likely to smoke pot and become addicted to other illicit substances such as heroin and cocaine later in their life.
3. The Chiefs are 12-12 against Chargers at Arrowhead stadium, but have been victorious in nine of the last eleven. The two teams have battled once in the playoffs, a 17-0 win by the Chargers in a 1992 First Round Playoff game in San Diego.
4. The San Diego Chargers have been searching for consistency this season, winning two, then losing one, winning two, then losing three, winning two, then losing 25-17 to Tampa Bay last week.
5. The Chargers possess one of the League's finest special teams unit. Kicker John Carney has made 25 of 29 field goals, going nine of 23 from 40 yards or more. Punter Darren Bennett is third in the NFL with a gross punting average of 46.7 yards.
6. Derrick Thomas recorded his fourth two-sack day of the season, making Chicago quarterback Dave Krieg the most sacked quarterback in NFL history with 485. Thomas has 15.5 of those sacks.
7. Marvelous Marcus Allen is currently tied with Walter Payton for the most rushing touchdowns scored in a career with 110. He is also tied with Roger Craig for the most pass receptions by a running back with 566. Allen has 31 rushing touchdowns as a Chief and needs three more to tie Ed Podolak (34) for the third most in team history. (Christian Okoye had 40 and Abner Haynes 39). Good luck Marcus, keep charging the top!

# Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim Nunnelly

**Kansas City Chiefs vs. Denver Broncos**

Sunday, Oct. 27, at 3 P.M. (Central)

**Mile High Stadium**

Channel 41 And Fox Radio (101.1 FM)

| MEMBER | RECORD | PREDICTION | REASON | WORLD SERIES CHAMPS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Charles Allen | 5-2 | Chiefs by 3 | Still the same! | Atlanta |
| Paul Booker | 5-2 | Denver by 6 | Elway has his way. | Atlanta |
| Carl Clark | 5-2 | Chiefs by 7 | K.C. home or away. | Atlanta |
| Ike Gardner | 6-1 | Denver by 10 | Bono plus Hackett, equal no offense. | Atlanta |
| Lloyd Greenfield | 5-2 | Denver by 3 | Altitude affects solidarity. | Atlanta |
| Ollie Hubbard | 4-2 | Chiefs by 6 | K.C. will score somebody. | Atlanta |
| Walt Livingston | 5-2 | Chiefs by 3 | Denver defense ailing. | Atlanta |
| Jim Nunnelly | 5-2 | Chiefs by 3 | Converted. | Atlanta |
| Harry Sypert | 3-4 | Denver by 7 | John El. | New York |
| Ed West | 5-2 | Denver by 3 | Superior QB! | Atlanta |

**Chatter, Conversation And Commentary**

1. Marvelous Marcus! During the Chiefs last game against the Seattle Seahawks, running back Marcus Allen scored his 108th and 109th rushing touchdowns. Allen, currently ranked second behind Walter Payton, needs only one more rushing touchdown to tie the Chicago Bear great. Keep humming Marcus, Kansas City fans are behind you all the way to the top.
2. Kansas City owns a 42-30 advantage in the series with the Broncos. This includes winning three straight games. The Chiefs have been victorious six of the last eight meetings. Fourteen of the last 18 games have been decided by eight points or less and four of the last six games have been decided by three points.
3. Last season the Chiefs led the NFL in scoring defense (15.1 points per game) and fewest offensive touchdowns allowed (23). This season Kansas City has the NFL's seventh-best scoring defense (15.4) and has allowed the second-fewest offensive touchdowns (eight). The Broncos lead the league in third-down conversion percentage (52.4 percent).
4. The Kansas City Chiefs have qualified for the playoffs six straight seasons, the longest current streak in the league.
5. On John Elway: When Elway passed for 326 yards against the Baltimore Ravens last Sunday for a 45-34 victory, he surpassed Warren Moon and moved into third place all-time in passing yards behind Fran Tarkenton and Dan Marino. He has faced the Kansas City Chiefs 25 times in his career, all at the regular season, and holds a 14-11 record against Them, including a 10-3 mark at Mile High stadium.
6. About 648 children are in foster homes at any one time in Jackson County. Children who experienced interruptive parenting are much more likely to use drugs later in life. A student at Park College is working on a paper tracing this subject.
7. About Terrell and Shannon: Terrell Davis' 194-yard rushing performance against the Baltimore Ravens not only set a personal high for him, it also broke the 22-year-old team record of 183 yards in a game set by Otis Armstrong. That performance also marked the eighth time in Davis' 21-game career that he has rushed for over 100 yards. Shannon Sharpe is among the leaders in receptions by a tight end in the 1990's, and in receptions by all offensive players since 1993. He also is among the leaders in receptions by yards in 1996. This year, Shannon Sharpe has had 48 receptions, 630 yards with five touchdown catches.

# CHATTER AND CONVERSATION

1. Seattle Seahawks running back Chris Warren topped the century mark for the first time in 1996 and the 22nd time in his career when the Hawks played the Green Bay Packers. Chris Warren has been selected to the Pro Bowl three times in his seven year NFL career.
2. Although the Seattle Seahawks are not sitting on top of the AFC standings, they are the "first" NFL team to have their tickets available through the internet. Their three home games thus far in the 1996 season have had an average attendance of 47,611.33 per game.
3. Marcus Allen scored one rushing touchdown in Kansas City's game against the Pittsburgh Steelers to move his all time touchdown rushing record to 107, just second behind Chicago Bear's great Walter Payton who holds the number one spot with 110.
4. Did you know? Any family needing assistance with a parent child issue may contact the Jackson County Family Court directly and on a voluntary basis without the necessity of legal intervention, to obtain counseling, mediation services or referral by calling 881-6570.
5. Watch out Kansas City! Here comes #89 Brian Blades and #96 Cortez Kennedy! Wide receiver Brian Blades and defensive Tackle Cortez Kennedy, both from Miami university, are key members of the Seahawk roster. At 5'11" and 190 pounds and a second round draft pick in 1988, Blades has been an integral part of the Seattle offense for his nine years in the NFL. Kennedy, who was a first round draft choice in 1990 for the Seahawks, is an agile 6'3", 306-pound tackle who will keep the Chiefs linemen scrambling.

# Commentary, Conversation and Chatter

## The Derrick vs. Neil Friendship

This game on Sunday is not about the Chiefs and their many new faces. And it’s not about the Chiefs’ flimsy, questionable offensive line.

It’s not even about Elway and his 26-touchdown pass season last year. Neither is it about Shannon Sharpe, Denver’s All-Pro tight end, who caught 80 of Elway’s bullets for 10 scores.

This game is about the special friendship Derrick Thomas and Neil Smith shared before the salary cap reality set in.

It all started about a decade ago, when Neil Smith showed up from Nebraska and Derrick Thomas came in from Alabama. One a tough, defensive lineman and the other a quick-of-foot linebacker. They didn’t know each other and they only would have the red and white uniform in common. But, they lined up together, talked a lot and went on to become the most feared sack duo in the entire National Football League, racking up 182 sacks together. Most of all, they became real good friends, even making a famous telephone long distance commercial together. Powerfully protective, and inseparable friendship, on and off the football field.

That is until the divisive, third party, impersonal salary cap entered. Last year, Derrick asked for and received a big contract from the front office. He even lobbied for his partner in crime, Neil Smith. The front office said no and sent Neil off to Denver to play for the Broncos. In the process, a wall had been built between the superstars.

But come this Sunday, that wall will come tumbling down. Derrick and Neil will line up on different sides, kind of like brother against brother. And for the first time in their starlike, professional careers. Though they won’t be on the field at the same time, they will battle each other in a different way.

Derrick will be chasing Denver quarterback John Elway. And Neil will be giving untested and untried Elvis Grbac a fit. Each watching and respecting each other. In spite of other buildups, this game will be about how many times Derrick gets to Elway and how many times Neil gets to Grbac. And they’ll be counting, too. It’ll be the “falcon” defense put on by Derrick against the post-sack homerun display, coined by Neil Smith when he was here in Kansas City.

And after the game, they’ll meet and greet at midfield. They’ll laugh, joke and talk and even hug. You’ll see Neil stretch out one of those long arms and you’ll see Derrick with that innocent, disarming smile. And we will all know that in spite of salary caps and short sighted front offices, that the Derrick versus Neil friendship is still intact.

By Jim Nunnelly

## **Next Game**

October 26, 1997

**Kansas City Chiefs vs. St. Louis Rams**

Predictions will resume next Friday, October 24

***Conversation, Chatter, and Commentary***

**LAST NIGHT'S GAME AT ARROWHEAD** -- Good to see Derrick Thomas and Lake Dawson in uniform and on the field.

**MONDAY NIGHT FOOTBALL -- OCTOBER 20,** Buffalo Bills play the winless Indianapolis Colts. Paul Booker, Ike Gardner, Jim Nunnelly and Ed West all think Indianapolis will win its first game. Carl Clark, Lloyd Greenfield, Walt Livingston, and Harry Sypert believe Buffalo will be victorious.

**SLASH WILL PLAY SUNDAY** -- Kordell Stewart, Pittsburgh Steeler quarterback, will play this Sunday. His knee was injured last week. Stewart used to be referred to as "Slash". That was last year when he played both quarterback and wide receiver. Though he retains the nickname, he is now a fulltime, full-fledged NFL quarterback.

**FISH THAT SAVED FLORIDA** -- The Florida Marlins are the first expansion team to make it to the World Series in just five years. The Marlins will play the Cleveland Indians tomorrow in the fall sports classic.

**MY HEART'S IN SAN FRANCISCO** -- Since Jerry Rice has been injured, the Forty Niners have won five consecutive games and now stand 5-1, the best record in the National Football Conference. The Kansas City Chiefs will play San Francisco at Arrowhead on November 30. Looks like another SuperBowl team. We shall see. We shall see.

**MISSOURI AND KANSAS TOMORROW** -- Look for the Missouri Tigers to barely beat the Texas Longhorns at its homecoming game tomorrow in Columbia. Meanwhile, the Kansas Jayhawks will travel to Boulder to face the Colorado Buffaloes. The Hawks should win, despite the altitude.

**K.C. JONES** -- You know he used to be the Boston Celtics' star player and coach. The Celtics' front office fired old K.C. and the NBA Hall of Famer is now coaching a women's team, the New England Blizzards.

**EMMITT, AIKMAN, AND IRVIN** -- With all that offensive talent, who would have thought the Dallas Cowboys would be 3-3 and struggling? Is it Barry's coaching style or is it Jerry Jones' ego interfering?

# Chatter, Conversation and Commentary

**1.** Marvelous Marcus is #1. On Thanksgiving Day, Marcus Allen soared his way to the top of the record books by scoring his 111th and 112th rushing touchdowns to assist in the Chiefs victory over the Detroit Lions. Allen previously held the rushing touchdown record with the Chicago Bears great Walter Payton at 110. Congratulations Allen and good luck breaking your own record many more times.

**2.** New Chiefs starting quarterback Rich Gannon debuted his versatile talents in fine fashion during the last game directing Kansas City to an important victory and keeping their home field wildcard playoff hopes alive.

**3.** Monday's game against the Oakland Raiders will be the 76th meeting between the two teams. This has become one of pro football's greatest rivalries which dates back to 1960, the initial American Football League season. The Chiefs barely lead the series, 37-36-1, making them one of only two teams (Philadelphia) in the NFL with a series edge over the Raiders.

**4.** The Raiders have back-to-back victories over the Seahawks (27-21) and Dolphins (17-7). The Raiders potent offense is led by Pro Bowl wide receiver Tim Brown. Currently, Brown is tied for second in the AFC with 75 catches for 915 yards and eight touchdowns.

**5.** Did you know? Chiefs running back Marcus Allen was a first-round draft choice of the Oakland Raiders 1982, but never played a game in the city before last season, moving with the team to Los Angeles before the start of the season. Allen was named Super bowl XVIII MVP following the 1983 season and was selected NFL MVP in 1985 after setting a league record for combined rushing and receiving yardage (2,314).

**6.** AFC West Update. The Denver Broncos (12-1) wrapped up the AFC West title and will have home field advantage throughout the playoffs. The Chiefs (9-4) are three games behind the Broncos and can qualify for their seventh straight playoff spot with a win over Oakland and losses by any of the following two teams: Indianapolis, Houston and San Diego. The Chiefs lead the NFL in most consecutive trips to the playoffs.

**7.** Niles Home for Children will be the beneficiary of an unusual gift this Christmas. The Monday Nite Football group will "give" Niles their time. During the next year, the group will volunteer and offer help where needed to the well-known children's facility. Ike Gardner, Paul Booker and Walt Livingston have worked at Niles through the years. Harry Sypert is employed there as an assistant to Don Lee, executive director.

**8.** Tony G, radio personality at the city's number one radio station KPRS, will begin his "Sleepless Marathon" today at Hypermart U.S.A. Tony G will collect food and toys for the needy, while staying awake during the entire week-end.

**9.** Cigarettes contain nicotine which is a drug. Exercising and participating in sports is nearly impossible if you smoke cigarettes. Also, you people who smoke cigarettes, are much more likely to smoke pot and become addicted to other illicit substances such as heroin and cocaine. Call COMBAT at 881-3814 for more information.

**Conversation, Chatter And Commentary**

**STEELER DOMINANCE** -- Pittsburgh has won all seven games played at Arrowhead. In fact, the Chiefs has lost to the Steelers more than any other AFC team, losing 14 times out of 20 games. You will remember last year in early October, when the Steelers spanked the Chiefs 17-7 in a nationally televised game. Look for history to repeat itself Monday night.

**MOON OVER OAKLAND** -- 41-year-old Seattle quarterback passed for 409 yards and five touchdowns last Sunday against Oakland. Moon has made the Pro Bowl eight times and was dealt to the Seahawks from Minnesota as a back-up behind John Friesz. He is closing in on the record for most 300 yards passing games. Back in 1990, Moon threw for 527 yards against the Chiefs at Arrowhead, setting a a new NFL passing record for a single game. Moon and the 5-3 Seahawks play again on November 23. The Seahawks could spell trouble for the Chiefs in their second half of the season. But this Sunday, Moon and the Seahawks go to Denver to match with Elway and the Broncos.

**MALE PRESENCE IN THE HOME** -- Researchers find that children from fatherless homes are more likely to become incarcerated, school dropouts, unemployed and totally uninvolved with their own children. Studies also show that mentoring programs fill this gap. If you know of some child who needs mentoring, call the COMBAT office at 881-3814.

# CONVERSATION, CHATTER, AND COMMENTARY

FAYE KIDD JACKSON, formerly of Kansas City and now from Oakland, sent a telegram to the Monday Niters over at Ed West's house Monday night. She stated that Oakland would "kick butt" and beat the Chiefs, 28-0. In addition, she called each one of the Monday Niters individually with the same message. Needless to say, she won't be answering her phone until the Christmas holidays. PLAYOFF NEMESIS BUFFALO has spoiled the Chiefs' chances several times in the recent past. In 1993, the Bills beat the Chiefs to stop them from going to the championship game. Then last year, the Chiefs needed only to beat the Bills, in order to get to the playoffs. The Bills again blocked the Chiefs, mainly behind the grossly inadequate play of Steve Bono, who threw two interceptions.

GREG HILL VS. THURMAN THOMAS: That's the running back matchup for Sunday. The Chiefs' speedy back is leading the AFC with a 6.3 yards per carry. He also is seventh in rushing with 145 yards in 23 carries. Thurman Thomas, on the other hand, has rushed for 1,000 yards or more for the last eight seasons. He will test the "falcon" defense, led by Derrick Thomas. Hill will win this match by gaining at least 120 yards on the ground plus another 40 in pass receptions.

DERRICK FOR 100: Derrick Thomas has 98 quarterback sacks, going into the game on Sunday. If he gets two against the Bills, he will join 15 other NFL greats such as Reggie White (!67). Derrick is due in spite of injuries. Where's the franchise player?

MONDAY NIGHT FOOTBALL, SEPTEMBER 15: Philadelphia plays at Dallas.

# Conversation, Chatter And Commentary

**REMAINING GAMES:** Here’s how the rest of the season stacks up for Denver and Kansas City. Denver leads the AFC West by one game over the Chiefs. Denver (9-2) has remaining: Oakland, San Diego, Pittsburgh and San Francisco. Kansas City (8-3) has remaining: Seattle, San Francisco, Oakland, San Diego and New Orleans.

**MOON AND SEATTLE SEAHAWKS:** You will remember Warren Moon has been around for quite a while. He’s the oldest, active quarterback, and Minnesota traded away the 41-year-old rifleman. After arriving at Seattle as a backup quarterback, he took over in the second game and has been "Number One" ever since. That doesn’t surprise us because ol’ Moon has thrown for more yardage than any other quarterback ever. Five years ago, Moon set a NFL record against the Chiefs in Arrowhead, throwing for 527 yards in one game. That makes us worry a little bit about this game. Moon now has a first class, wide receiver in Joey Galloway and the duo has helped Seattle to stay in the play-off running. Look for ol’ Moon to fill the air with passes on Sunday.

**MONDAY NIGHT FOOTBALL:** November 24 -- The Denver Broncos, fresh from Kansas City loss, will face the only other team to beat them this year, the Oakland Raiders (28-25). Oakland could possibly hand Denver its third defeat. The Chiefs obviously will be pulling for Oakland.

**WELFARE GENDER INEQUITY:** All of the attention is being paid to welfare mothers and getting them off AFDC. Little attention is being paid to the father side of the issue. Look for an exciting new project addressing this issue. If you know of someone -- male or female -- who wants to help. Give a call to Jim Nunnelly at COMBAT, 881-3814 or Velda Cook, 881-3812.

Neil Smith, former Chiefs defensive end, has 8.5 sacks for Denver this year. The only loss for the Broncos this year was against Oakland 28-25. **ABOUT KANSAS CITY** -- Andre Rison is having a Pro-Bowl season, catching 50 passes already for fourth in the AFC. Look for Denver's Atwater and Braxton to really make it hard for Rison. Greg Hill, despite a lot of criticism, is 19th in AFC rushing. Kicker Pete Stoyanovich has made 17 out of 18 field goals. He's out to make us forget about Nick Lowery.

**MONDAY NIGHT FOOTBALL, NOV. 17** -- For once, all the Monday Niters think that Miami will beat the declining Buffalo Bills.
