# **Monday Nite Footballers Predictions**
By Jim Nunnelly

**Pre-Season Game 4**
**Governor's Cup**
**Kansas City Chiefs (1-2) vs. St. Louis Rams (1-2)**
**Trans World Dome, St. Louis, Mo.**
**Friday, August 22, 1997**
**Kick-Off 7:00 P.M.**
**WDAF-TV Channel 4 and KCFX-FM 101.1**

**CHATTER, CONVERSATION AND COMMENTARY**

Three-time Pro-Bowl cornerback Dale Carter remains one of the NFL's top two or three corners. And cornerback James Hasty is not that far behind. Others in the mix include Mark McMillian, who started 60 games for the Eagles and Saints for the past four years. The Chiefs are solid at this position and if you add in Tony Stargell and Darren Anderson, someone has to go.

Elvis Grbac begins his first season as a full-time starter in the NFL. His only pre-season action came on the first series of the Steelers' game, when he was five of six for 45 yards on a touchdown drive. Since then he has suffered an abdomen injury and has not played since. The jury is still out on Elvis. That's because we have not seen him under game conditions. But Rich Gannon has showed great promise as a backup. Again, the Chiefs have two backup quarterbacks and we'll just have to wait and see on the quarterback situation. Don't expect too much.

For the first time since the 1993 season, the Chiefs will have a new tailback. Greg Hill. He replaces Marcus Allen, who broke several NFL records, including most touchdowns scored by a running back. Greg finally gets his chance after waiting for Marcus to break the touchdown record. Now, Greg will step in and make the fans forget Marcus. He will add speed, quickness, and a lot of yards to a rather dull and questionable backfield.

Of course, Kimble Anders will play his usual quiet but effective game. You can look for him to lead Greg Hill to his first 1,000-yard season since being at Texas. Speaking of blocking, that brings up the rebuilt offensive line. Grunhard and Shields will be okay. But, don't look for a lot from Szott and Jenkins. In short, the blocking will be better in the backfield. However, should this group jell, Hill could even sprint 1,400 yards.

The receiver corps is strong individually. Andre Rison and Brett Perriman will help us over the middle. And the aging Lake Dawson seems ready to play. Certainly, first round pick, Tony Gonzalez is a sure bet for the Rookie of the Year in the AFC. However, good this group may be, we still have to consider the King Grbac and how good his arm really is. Fans, we have the receivers...but do we have the passer and the blockers?

So, what does it all mean? Last year, ol' Ike Gardner was right when he said the highly rated Super Bowl picked Chiefs would go only 9-7. This year, Ike says they will win only seven and lose nine. Paul Booker, mayor of the 6902, thinks the Chiefs will settle in at 8-8. Don't pay any attention to optimists like Carl Clark and Dr. Charles Allen. The Chiefs will be lucky to escape a losing season, let alone qualify for the playoffs. In short, this is not the year that the Chiefs' sundial comes out of the shade. Do like I am going to do...wait till next year.

# Monday Nite Footballers Predictions
By Jim Nunnelly

## Regular Season Game 15
**Kansas City Chiefs (11-3)**
vs.
**San Diego Chargers (4-10)**
3 P.M., Sunday, December 14
Qualcomm Stadium, San Diego, Cal.
KSHB-TV41 And KCFX-FM 101.1

| MEMBER | PREDICTION | COMMENT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Charles Allen (11-3) | Chiefs by 10 | 12 game momentum building! |
| Paul Booker (4-10) | Chiefs by 3 | I'm jumping ship! |
| Carl Clark (10-4) | Chiefs by 14 | Same O, Same O! |
| Ike Gardener (6-8) | Chiefs by 10 | I'm a believer, NOW! |
| Lloyd Greenfield (10-4) | Chiefs by 7 | How you like me now? |
| Ollie Hubbard (10-4) | Chiefs by 3 | For the good times! |
| Walt Livingston (10-4) | Chiefs by 20 | Defense is razor sharp! |
| Jim Nunnelly (9-5) | Chiefs by 21 | Long runs and pass! |
| Harry Sypert (3-11) | San Diego by 3 | The luck is over! |
| Ed West (11-3) | Chiefs by 10 | Tune up time! |

**Conversation, Chatter And Commentary**

**ABOUT THE CHIEFS** -- In the last six games, the Chiefs defense has recorded 32 sacks, five interceptions and six fumble recoveries. They've only allowed about 15 points per game, good enough for second in the NFL in scoring defense. Whatever you may think, the defense "is" the difference. Against San Diego, the Chiefs have won the last five out of seven games, including a 31-3 pounding at Arrowhead on October 16. Andre Rison caught eight passes and scored two touchdowns in that well-coached, nationally televised game. Speaking of coaches, Ol' Marty has now coached 12 straight teams to winning seasons and nine of those teams had 10 wins or more. Are we really Super Bowl bound? Ed West, Dr. Charles Allen, Carl Clark and Donna Stewart says yes!

**ABOUT SAN DIEGO** -- The 4-10 San Diego Chargers have lost their last six games. Thus, they will be ready to upset anyone, especially Kansas City. Gary Brown, the Chargers leading rusher, will probably test the Chiefs defense again. Third string quarterback Craig Whellan will start his fourth straight game in place of the two injured quarterbacks. Linebacker Junior Seau will go after his seventh Pro Bowl appearance against the Chiefs offense. Those of you who remember former St. Louis Cardinal running back Terry Metcalf will get a special treat; his son Eric has returned six punts for touchdowns for San Diego.

**MONDAY NIGHT FOOTBALL** -- Jerry Rice, famed wide receiver, will play for San Francisco against the Denver Broncos. If Denver loses and Kansas City wins, it will put the Chiefs in sole possession of first place in the AFC West.

| Paul Booker (4-5) | Chiefs by 3 | Determined! |
| Carl Clark (8-1) | Chiefs by 7 | Time to take it on the road. |
| Ike Gardner (5-4) | Jacksonville by 3 | Chiefs' luck runs out. |
| Lloyd Greenfield (7-2) | Chiefs by 7 | Just a solid team. |

| Jim Nunnelly (7-2) | Chiefs by 2 | Hill is the real deal. |

| Ed West (8-1) | Chiefs by 7 | Defense and Derrick. |

**Conversation, Chatter And Commentary**

**JACKSONVILLE JAGUARS** -- Sunday's game will be the first for the Chiefs (7-2) against Jacksonville (6-3). Turnovers may play an important role in the game. Jacksonville Jaguars opened the season with three wins and have split the last six. Last week, they beat the Baltimore Ravens 30-24 after falling to the Steelers and the Dallas Cowboys. Mark Brunnel is the quarterback. James Stewart and Natrone Means are the running backs.

**THE STEELER DOMINANCE ENDED** -- For the first time in eight games, the Chiefs overtook Pittsburgh in Arrowhead. Jerome (Big Bus) Bettis just walked through the Chiefs defense for 103 rushing yards. This Sunday's is starting quarterback, Rich Gannon, threw 5 for 5 passes in that game. The Chiefs offense overall is ranked 11th in the NFL, recording 328 yards per game.

**DERON CHERRY** -- Kansas City Hall of Famer is a partner with the Jacksonville Jaguars.

**COMING UP** -- After visiting the Jaguars, the Chiefs will face Denver at Arrowhead on Sunday, Nov. 16. The next week is at Seattle and then return here to face the "hot" San Francisco Forty Niners.

| Paul Booker | Chiefs by 13 | I must redeem myself. |
| Carl Clark | Chiefs by 10 | Chiefs will jell. |

**Chatter, Conversation And Commentary**

**ASSESSING THE CHIEFS' RUNNING BACKS:** Right now, Donnell Bennett is averaging 83 yards per game rushing the ball. He's had one touchdown and his longest run is 26 yards. Tony Richardson spends most of his time blocking, the task Bennett had last year. Rookie Rashaan Shehee has not been a factor, gaining only 12 yards in four attempts. Kimble Anders has only gained a mere 38 yards but has a touchdown. Overall, this group has not improved our situation as promised.

**CHIEFS SUPER SPORTS SUNDAY ON RADIO 890:** Join us to talk about the Philadelphia/Kansas City game at 4 p.m. on radio 890-AM. It's a call-in show and you'll have a chance to talk directly to Monday Niters about their predictions. Matt Clay, the usual host, will be covering the game live in Philadelphia.

**LOOK FOR DERRICK THOMAS:** Falcon linebacker Derrick Thomas will be all over the Philadelphia Eagles. If you think the Chiefs have had offensive problems, let's look at the Eagles. They've only totaled 258 yards a game. To make matters worse, their offensive line allowed a sack for every seven pass attempts. That's among the "worst" in the league. And that should allow Derrick to increase his league-leading sack total.

**ANOTHER THOUGHT ON FATHERING:** Research shows that fathers who are involved with their children take fewer risks, i.e. driving fast, drinking, drugging, crime, etc. Thus, to be involved is not only good for the children, it is good for the fathers as well. That's the reason the Jackson County Prosecutor is sponsoring a project on Fathering. Call Velda Cook, 881-3812, or Jim Nunnelly, 881-3814, for more information.

# Conversation, Chatter, and Commentary

**ABOUT THE NEW ENGLAND PATRIOTS:** Drew Bledsoe still leads the offense at quarterback, throwing five touchdowns already this young season. And his receivers are rated i.e. Terry Glenn, Shawn Jefferson, Troy Brown, and Vincent Bagby. Running back Robert Edwards rounds out the well balanced offense. On the defensive side, end Willie McGinest and cornerback Ty Law will haunt the Chiefs on the ground and in the air. The return men, Troy Brown (punts) and Cullors (kickoffs) will also test the Chiefs' shaky special teams.

**AND ABOUT THOSE CHIEFS:** Marty says there is no quarterback controversy. Grbac is the starter and Gannon is the backup, no matter what Gannon has done since Grbac broke his collar bone way back in the opening Oakland game. But the fans are all clamoring for Gannon because they believe the "rhythm" will be affected if Grbac is reinserted. I agree that no one should lose the job because of injury and Marty is appropiate in awarding Elvis his job back...on principle alone. But, if Grbac doesn't cut it, he'll have no problem in reinserting Gannon. We all have our memory of Gannon throwing that 80-yard bomb to Andre Rison. And for now, that's all we'll have is a memory.

**MAYOR ASKS IKE TO SUPPORT CHIEFS:** Right before his sermon at St James United Methodist church, Mayor Emanuel Cleaver called his parishoners' attention to Ike Gardner's sometimey support for the Kansas City Chiefs. Everyone was surprised that the Mayor even read this column, let alone read it close enough to detect that one of his faithful members would be unsupporting of the Chiefs. While the Mayor was conducting the inside activities, Sandra Inez Hubbard was outside the church placing flyers on cars, wishing Ike a "93rd" birthday. Though Ike is in retirement, he's not 93. It was all a practical joke being played on the maverick-thinking Ike.

# Monday Nite Footballers Predictions
By Jim Nunnelly

# Chiefs’ Defense May Pave Way to Miami

Carl Clark, Ike Gardner and Lloyd Greenfield saw it for themselves. They were among the first Kansas Citians to see the 1998 edition of the Kansas City Chiefs. They all went down to Norman, Okla., to see last week’s pre-season contests against the renewed, Tampa Bay Bucs. These Monday Niters will agree that all is well with the defense. Since last week we analyzed the “Midwest Coast” offense, this week will look at this “Super Bowl” defense upclose and see what makes it tick(or shall we say hum?) After that, we”ll give you an overall assessment that you can believe and trust.

**DEFENSIVE LINE:** You’ve already heard the names of Dan Williams, Vaughn Booker and John Browning. They’re back and proud from last year’s star-studded defensive line crew. This year, you will hear some additional names like Chester McGlockton and Leslie O’Neal. All are quick, agile and mean and represent one of the best defensive lines in the entire NFL. And, if you add standup, “falcon” linebacker Derrick Thomas to that awesome line-up, then this group can handle any running back or pass rush placed before them. RATING: Excellent and deep.

**DEFENSIVE BACKS:** Carter, Hasty, McMillian, Tongue, and Woods. The last names don’t belong to a law firm or a court room. But they will have the task of keeping receivers in check. It seems only yesterday they were all experiments and youthful. Today, they are headed to the Pro Bowl and most important, they will do their part in paving the Chiefs’ way to the Super Bowl in Miami.
RATING: As Good As They Come

**LINEBACKERS:** Fast, deep, tough, and capable. That describes Donnie Edwards, Anthony Davis, and Wayne Simmons. These swift giants are the best linebackers to ever put on a Chiefs’ uniform (that includes Willie Lanier, Jim Lynch and Bobby Bell). You’ll hear national tv commentators go on and on about this trio. And you’ll witness them forcing Denver, Arizona, Jacksonville and Philadelphia into third and long situations. I kid you not; this group will go down as the greatest threesome in the NFL. RATING: None Better Anywhere

**OVERALL ASSESSMENT:** Marty Schottenheimer may be criticized for not winning the big one. And it is true he has had post-season difficulties. However so, Marty is often overlooked for his real contribution to this area and the entire NFL. As a onetime linebacker himself, Marty truly believes that defenses win ball games, by keeping the other team in check and recovering fumbles and forcing interceptions. That logic has led him to always draft, and put the defense first. Thus, his real contribution is that he has almost made us believe in this philosophy as well. And, this will be the year Marty will be crowned for

# Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim "Granddad" Nunnelly

**Pittsburgh Steelers (4-2)**
Vs.
**Kansas City Chiefs (4-2)**

Arrowhead Stadium, October 26, At 7 P.M.

**KMBC-TV 9 and KCFX-FM**

| MEMBER | PREDICTION | COMMENT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Allen, Charles | Chiefs by 7 | Thomas in force. |
| Booker, Paul | Steelers by 10 | Kordell will pass 'em crazy. |
| Clark, Carl | Chiefs by 1 | Defense will rise again. |
| Gardner, Ike | Chiefs by 1 | Pride and the home crowd. |
| Greenfield, Lloyd | Chiefs by 3 | Waiting to exhale. |
| Hubbard, Ollie | Chiefs by 6 | I can only say "holy cow!" |
| Livingston, Walt | Chiefs by 3 | Both have sputtering offenses. |
| Nunnelly, Jim | Chiefs by 1 | Rison or Shehee become stars. |
| Sypert, Harry | Steelers by 6 | "Bus" runs over the Chiefs. |
| West, Ed | Chiefs by 7 | Black on black crime. |

**Chatter, Conversation, and Commentary**

**ABOUT THE STEELERS:** Pittsburgh is best known for big back, Jerome Bettis, better known as "The Bus." He's gained just over 400 yards in 90 attempts. Before the national television audience, Bettis will give the Chiefs defense a fit. Kordell Stewart, the double threat quarterback, has thrown for three touchdowns so far. But, he's also dished out seven interceptins making several important writers doubt his ability this year. Don't believe it. Worth noting is the Steelers' All-Pro Center, Dermonti Dawson, who will open up the Chiefs sometimey defense. Speaking of defense, Carnell Lake and a decent corps of linebackers will give the Chiefs' sometimey offense the blues.

**KUDOS FOR THE BREAKFAST BROTHERS:** This past Wednesday, Myron D. and Sonny Andre, best known as the "breakfast brothers," chose as their morning topic, "When do you tell your kids about drugs." The pair are morning hosts for the HOT 103 radio program. They offered parents an opportunity to tell about their family strategies in dealing with a major public health problem in our city. As COMBAT administrator, I would add that we must keep our children insulated and inoculated against drugs by keeping them busy every moment of every day...in recreational, educational, and spiritual activities. Again, our congrats to the brothers.

**ABOUT THE CHIEFS:** James Hasty and Dale Carter will have a great game this Monday, tackling and intercepting the Steelers frequently. Despite his recent disappearances, Derrick Thomas will get a couple of key sacks. Most important, Andre Rison and rookie Shehee will get the "big play" awards. Linebackers Donnie Edwards and Anthony Davis will do well on third downs. And the ever reliable Pete Stoyanovich will come through in the narrow victory.

**WILLIAM JACKSON ON 890:** KCTV-TV 5's sportscaster William Jackson will guest on Mac Clay's talk show on KGGN-AM this Sunday at 4 p.m. The pre-game show will air the day before the actual game.

# Monday Nite Footballers Predictions
## By Jim 'Grand Dad' Nunnélly

## PRE-SEASON HOME OPENER SUNDAY

The Tennessee Titans, the renamed and relocated Houston Oilers, will visit Arrowhead stadium this Sunday evening to help the Chiefs celebrate their preseason opener. Look for a near sell-out, but don't look for anything too revealing. Starters or veterans will play a quarter or so and then defer to the rookies, free agents, and also-rans. But do lookout for Warren Moon, arguably the best backup quarterback in the NFL. Moon will play a lot, mainly to get acquainted with the Chiefs "Midwest Coast Offense." Moon, who has filled the air with rifle-like passes for many years, was chosen to become second fiddle to shaky Elvis Grbac. Knowing the 41-year-old veteran, he will add some sparks to an offense in need of something.

## SIGNIFICANT POTPOURRI

During all the hoopla surrounding Denver's second consecutive Super Bowl victory, little attention was given to the losing team, the Atlanta Falcons, and its classy running back, Jamil Anderson. Last year, Anderson carried the ball a record 410 times, making him the busiest back in the entire NFL. That and his 1,800-plus yards earned him a five-year contract worth a cool $32 million... You will remember Brianna Scurry, game-saving goalie of the world champion U.S. Soccer team. She and her teammates will have their individual pictures featured on the Wheaties breakfast cereal box. Though not the first time for an African American female, it will be the first time for soccer players to be so honored.

## A TRIVIA QUESTION

What do the San Diego Chargers, the Denver Broncos, and the Pittsburgh Steelers have in common? **ANSWER:** They are all on the Chiefs' 1999 regular season schedule and each has been to the Super Bowl in the last five years, Denver winning twice and Pittsburgh and San Diego losing to Dallas and San Francisco, respectively.

## 'A WORD FROM GRAND DAD' ON GENERATION RAP

The word tomorrow will be "clandestine." Current events make us consider the power and application of this word. All of us know about the shootings in Atlanta and this week's hate assault on the Jewish Community center. We'll discuss how underground, secretive people emerge in the headlines. It's all on Carl Boyd's "Generation Rap" at 8 a.m. Saturday on the HOT 103 KPRS-FM.

## GLOSSARY OF FOOTBALL TERMINOLOGY

As the high school, college and professional football seasons start, there will be terms being used that may be unfamiliar to you. While the list is not exhaustive, it does include some you will hear used by commentators and officials. We, Monday Niters, want to start you off with the right stuff:

**AUDIBLE** -- This is a change of play, shouted out by the quarterback at the line of scrimmage. Do not confuse this with the usual calling of the signals.
**BLITZ** -- This is a pass rush conducted by the defensive backfield. Since the line usually rushes, it is considered an expected surprise.
**RED ZONE** -- The area inside the 20-yard line. In short, if the offense moves the ball to the red zone, they are expected to score a touchdown.
**SACK** -- When the quarterback is tackled behind the line of scrimmage, while attempting to pass.
**SHOTGUN** -- An offensive formation where the quarterback lines up five to seven yards behind the line of scrimmage, usually to pass.

If you have a question about the Chiefs, football in general, or drug problems, call Jim Nunnelly at 881-3814 and leave a message.

## COMBAT KUDOS

Tracey Blaylock and Angela Castle deserve congratulations for their efforts in kicking off "Drug Awareness Month" at the Pioneer College campus at 18th and Prospect this past Saturday. Ms. Blaylock is a manager at the Mid-America Addiction Technology Transfer Center and Ms. Castle is employed by Jackson County Prosecutor Bob Beaird.

# Monday Nite Footballers Predictions
By Jim “Grand Dad” Nunnelly

**Denver Broncos**
**vs.**
**Kansas City Chiefs**
**Sunday, Sept. 19, 1999, 3:15 p.m.**

**Channel 5 and 101 KCFX-FM**

# CHATTER, CONVERSATION AND COMMENTARY

Carl Clark said it best Monday night. "The Chiefs just ain't ready." While we can forgive Carl's English, we can't forgive the Chiefs and their continued use of Grbac at quarterback. Ed West says it's Gunther's fault that we keep Grbac in spite of the team's inability to operate with him. The only good thing about Sunday's game was everyone else in the AFC West lost as well. Gannon, who should really be leading the Chiefs right now, has found a welcoming committee out in Oakland. Look for him to raise the silver and platinum to contemporary idols. While we still insist that Grbac is the one, Ike Gardner blames the Chicago loss solely on him and thinks the Chiefs will lose again Sunday for the very same reason. Las Vegas rates the Broncos as favorites even though Denver is totally lost without Elway. Following that logic, almost everyone in Monday Night Football is predicting the Chiefs' second loss on Sunday. Here's what they say:

# PREDICTIONS

| MEMBER | PREDICTION | REASON |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Charles Allen | Chiefs by 3 | Just wait till it's over |
| Paul Booker | Denver by 7 | Terrell Davis will run |
| Carl Clark | Denver by 14 | Chiefs ain't ready |
| Ike Gardner | Denver by 17 | Elvis Grbac |
| Lloyd Greenfield | Chiefs by 3 | Gunther |
| Ollie Hubbard | Denver by 10 | Three-blind mice |
| Jim Nunnelly | Chiefs by 1 | 75,000 fans |
| Ed West | Denver by 20 | Schottenheimer |

# ABOUT DENVER

As stated earlier, Denver is severely weakened by the absence of its retired, great quarterback, John Elway. Brian Griese is not ready yet. But, they do have Terrell Davis, who is two times faster when he's being chased. You will remember that he ran for over 2,000 yards last year, becoming only the fourth player in NFL history to accomplish such. And he takes every game as if it's his first. In addition, Denver is deep with receivers. There's Shannon Sharpe, Rod Smith and emerging Ed McCaffrey, who always seems to be open for some reason. So look for Denver to run and pass when they can. Hopefully the Chiefs' defense can stop this new offense before it gets started. It will be a close one and look for the Chiefs' crowd to help them out and take the AFC West lead.

# SIGNIFICANT POTPOURRI

Rita Booker is back in town from California. She assisted her mother in preparing for the 30th kickoff of the Monday Nite Footballers...Beverly Allen is now Director of Special Education in the Hickman Mills school district.

# Monday Nite Footballers Predictions
**By Jim "Grand Dad" Nunnelly**

# Detroit Lions
## Vs.
# Kansas City Chiefs
**Sunday, Sept. 26, at 12 Noon**
**Arrowhead Stadium**
**Fox 4 And 101 KCFX-FM**

# Chatter, Conversation and Commentary

Paul Booker spoke up this week about the Chiefs' game with the Detroit Lions. He warns that "Kansas City should button down the hatch, because the boys from Motown are here with their star, Charlie Hatch." That means that the Chiefs will have trouble containing the fleet-footed young quarterback Hatch. He has replaced Barry Sanders and the hope of Detroit. He's completed 70 percent of his passes and conducts the offense in a seasoned way. Lions coach Bobby Ross says Hatch ranks well with any other quarterback in the NFL. In short, Ross and Paul Booker are counting on the youthful signal-caller to keep the Lions undefeated. In fact, some Detroiters are thinking Super Bowl, something that even Barry Sanders did not deliver.

And Kansas City will be tested, even more so than with Denver. Kimble Anders is gone for the year. And everyone is still crossing their fingers when they talk of Elvis Grbac. The only real hope the locals have is their dynamic big-play wide receiver, Derrick Alexander. All Grbac needs to do is get the ball somewhere in the vicinity of Alexander and he will run for additional yards. Alexander is important this week for sure, since Gunther Cunningham's run-he-ball offense has been weakened. Look out for running backs Shehee and Cloud to surprise fans with their lightning speed. While Booker is insightfully poetic, the Chiefs should rally and win a close one.

# Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

It’s the third year of the Dick Vermeil era. The Chiefs’ offense, the best in the NFL last year, is in tact. Their defense has been beefed up, led by Shawn Barber, Ryan Sims, and the returning Jerome Woods. And except for cantankerous Ike Gardner, we all believe the Chiefs will win the season opener against San Diego, Sunday out at Arrowhead.

But, Ike is Ike. Even his closest friends don't understand why he has taken such an opposing view, when it comes to the Chiefs. Understandably, he is a frustrated, loyal Chiefs’ fan. In fact, his season ticket seats are on the 50-yard-line, directly behind the Chiefs’ bench. That way, he can harass, meddle with, and help coach the Chiefs. Sincerely though, Ike just wants the Chiefs to return to the old glory days. And, it has been 33 years since the Chiefs have been to the Super Bowl. Thus, Ike represents a group of old-timers, who just want the Chiefs to compete well and be respected, again.

Because he has been disappointed so many times, he has lost faith and just wants the Chiefs to win those games and not just give them away.

So, next week, Monday Niters will all look into the future, and predict just how many games the Chiefs will win in 2003. That discussion will take place this Monday night, just as it has for the last 34 years. Ten friends, sitting down together, eating a kingly-prepared meal, taking a quick nap, and just talking endlessly about their favorite pastime, football. Even, Ike joins in, occasionally.

**Editor’s Note:** Of course, Ike continues to give up on our beloved Chiefs even before they take their first series in the new season. Well, ol' Ike, someday you'll learn. Of course the real “Chiefs” faithful will show you old-timer just how good this 2003 bunch will be. Come on Ike, give us some love.

# Monday Nite Footballers
By Jim "Granddad" Nunnelly
Predicting Since 1970

# Kansas City Chiefs
# At
# Oakland Raiders
# 3:05 p.m., KCTV 5

# Chatter, Conversation And Commentary
Well, well, well. We've reached that irreversible period in our season when the Chiefs can no longer talk about playoff hopes. The Chiefs are not going to win, show or place in the AFC West Division. Like breathing, losing has become an expected thing. In fact, the honorable Earl Abel keeps asking me why I remain loyal to the Chiefs, and expect- ing them to win when it is obvious that losing is their fate. So what, then, do we talk about in the meantime? Well, well. We could talk about the Chiefs' history when they "were" winning. Or, we could talk about the NFL teams that are winning. Or, we could just switch sports altogether and talk about Mizzou basketball.
Let's do all three.
First, about the Chiefs' glory days. Most of us are not as old as cantankerous Ike Gardner. So, remembering the Super Bowl days would be fruitless. But, a fact came up in our meeting over at Charles Allen's house. That is, the Chiefs were 13-3 in both the 1995 and 1997 seasons. Surprised? We even checked it out with the Chiefs' official records and even conferred with Big M over at Seven Oaks. True Dat. And in case you are wondering who the starting quarterbacks were ... in 1995, it was Steve Bono, and in 1997, it was Elvis Grbac and really, Rich Gannon. Grbac led the team to a 7-2 begin- ning and was hurt on a Monday night game. Gannon took over and went 6-1, right up to the playoffs. Then, the coached returned Grbac to the starting rôle against Denver. And we lost, touching off the long-term discussion about who should be the starter. That playoff loss left the Chiefs with a lot of egg on their faces and everyone turned their attention to other teams.
Speaking of other teams this season. Oakland, under Gannon's guidance, will win the AFC West. And could go all the way with Jerry Rice and Tim Brown making acro- batic catches. Miami seems representative. And the Pittsburgh Steelers will play in the Super Bowl come February. With or without Jerome "the Bus" Pettis. St. Louis will do all right and you can look for Green Bay to be in the thick of things. And who would have thought the Chicago Bears would be leading the NFC Central? Obviously, we didn't mention the Chiefs for they will be home, looking into on for the future. Maybe, even watching college basketball.
College basketball, you say? How about those University of Missouri Tigers? Now 9-0, they have shown they can win, even those close games in the last seconds. Highly touted Iowa fell to the Tigers in the last seconds, as did Saint Louis university. The quintet of Rush, Gilbert, Johnson, Paulding and Stokes can dominate the Big 12 just like they have in the pre-conference outings. Look for a tough game against Ohio next week and don't forget the Kansas Jayhawks, longtime nemesis for the Tigers. Quin Snyder and his quintet has given us a winning team to talk about . . . something the Chiefs can't do for us right now or anytime soon. MIZZOU!!!

# Monday Nite Footballers Commentary
By Jim Nunnelly

**College Basketball's Three Pointer: Change And Motion**

Well, 1986 will be remem-
bered as the "year of the
change in sports." The
Gardner and the Monday
Niters saw the instant re-
play become a reality in
pro football. And now from
Dallas, the NCAA has al-
lowed a three-point play
in college basketball. What
about this change, or is it
just movement for motion's
sake. Let's examine the
potential effects.

First off and most im-
portant, it will put the 6-
footer back in the college
basketball game. Conser-
vative coaches, like Norm
Stewart of Missouri, will
have to "move" the game
from the "paint" to the
"arch." Outside sharp-
shooters can now have their
territorial markings on the
court. Fans like Tom Brown
welcome this aspect. But
let's look closer at college
recruiting and how that
may change.

Well, those same college
coaches who go from sea
to sea to recruit the seven
footer, the six niner, will
now "blend and spend"
time paying some attention
to the lowly high school
guards—those that just han-
dle the ball well and pop
a few from the top of the
key.

And, really the 19-foot,
9-inch distance from the
center of the basket is just
not that far. And of course,
the motion of the game
will change too. And here's
where only the up close
fans (like Monday Nites)
will really see the effects.
Picture late in a tight
game. Instead of a keen

defensive-minded special-
ist entering the game, you
will now see the sharp
shooter line up at the shoot-
er’s table. Where only
layups used to be permit-
ted, now the three-point
arch will draw all the at-
tention. And maybe that's
what we want. But, there
are still questions about
all of this. What are some?

Is the NCAA just pro-
tecting its share of TV re-
venues by implementing
such a bold change? Do
the college fans really want
to see everything dupli-
cated from pro sports? Will
missing or making a three
pointer make or ruin the
chances for professional
admiration?

And what about the spill-
over effect—will we see
an instant replay rule in
college football in the years
to come? And will high
school adopt a 24-second
clock?

Like everyone else, we
Monday Niters don't know
for sure. We only know
that the world of sports
is constantly changing and
it won't be long before all
rules will in some way be
relaxed. Some will like it,

others will hate it. But,
that's change for you.

All we can say right now
is all change is not growth
and all movement is not
forward. Let's wait it out
a the arch.

It's something to think
about.

Monday Niters members
are: Charles Allen, D.D.S.,
Jim Nunnelly, Ollie Hub-
bard, C. Ashley, Issac
Gardner, Ed West, Ter-
ry Price, Carl Clark, Paul
Booker and Tom Brown.

# Monday Nite Footballers
**Commentary**
**By Jim Nunnelly**

# Rozelle Takes Stand For New Victims

Last week, we promised that we would continue to weigh and balance the use of drugs by professional athletes. And supporting that goal were Paul Booker, Carl Clark, Ollie Hubbard, Jerry Price and Ed West. All felt drug testing was the right thing to do, especially when two topranked athletes met their deaths because of cocaine.

Well, we hardly got the words out of our mouths before Pete Rozelle, the commissioner of the National Football League, released a drug testing plan — for all NFL players. What’s in the plan and how are others viewing it?

First, every football player will be drug tested three times a year — once in training camp and twice randomly throughout the regular season. And, unlike other drug testing programs, this plan will be supervised by a NFL-hired physician. What a bold move! No longer will the teams be in charge of the testing — the League office will be. Go ahead, Rozelle.

But, just why did Rozelle “harden” the NFL’s position. He had already agreed to a plan approved by the Players Association sometime ago. So why did he “get tougher” in the middle of the stream. Did the two deaths get him to react bolder? No, Rozelle says he’s worried about all the victims caused by drug users. Not only the athletes who use drugs, but also “those injured by those taking drugs”. Talk about a new victim.

At first defense for this new “victim” seems a “little loaded” and farfetched. But, when you talk to those who are victimized by drug users on the field, the issue is not so “way out”.

After talking with a few agents, we are impressed that there is absolute fear among those who don’t, of those who do not use drugs. Drug-clean athletes are expressing concern that they cannot compete fairly against those “hopped up” athletes.

Not only are the victims physically abused by the drug-inspired athlete, but when the victims are exhausted after playing three tough quarters, the “inspired” athletes are still playing as if it were the opening kickoff.

Well, whatever your position, it’s clear that Pete Rozelle is no longer willing to “pussyfoot” with those who use drugs. He’s coming down real hard and the message is clear — “papa don’t want no drug using in here”.

And in a rather creatively informative (some judgementally opinionated) way, Rozelle has enlightened the public by addressing the concerns of those who have to play against those who use drugs. It is certainly a step in the right direction.

Now, where to next. Let’s keep exploring.

Members of the Monday Nite Footballers are; Charles Allen, D.D.S., C. Ashley, Tom Brown, Paul Booker, Carl Clark, Ike Gardner, Ollie Hubbard, Jim Nunnelly, Jerry Price and Ed West.

# Monday Nite Footballers Commentary
## By Jim Nunnelly

**Bobby Sumler: Working...Graceful...Lucky?**

Diligence may well be the mother of good luck. That old saying is true and may well apply to Bobby Sumler. That's the little floor general from the Washburn Ichabods, winners of the NAIA Tournament Tuesday night at Kemper. The Topeka-based college outlasted West Virginia State, 79-77.

But before we get into Bobby's on-the-court contributions, let's look inside Bobby Sumler. First, he's from San Diego, and he kind of likes the change Topeka offers. A Communications major, he doesn't know there is "life after the NAIA championship."

And he even encourages his younger sister to choose the right college and do well. Now, let's get back to his court performance. And see just how his diligence may pay off for him.

**HE DID HIS THING**
On Tuesday, 8,137 in-house and millions of ESPN television fans watched Bobby steal the show. He wasn't the highest scorer; he didn't win the Most Valuable Player trophy; and he didn't lead the team in rebounding.

But he was diligent, nonetheless. He did score 18 points, second highest. And he handed off a dozen assists. And he connected three out of four times from the three-point arch. Yes, he dazzled fans and pro scouts alike, performing with the grace of an Olympic figure skater. Bobby was "the show from Topeka." Working to shoot. Working to pass. Working to win.

**WON HUSTLE AWARD**
That kind of working earned him the coveted Charles Stevenson Hustle award a place on the All-tourney's second team. Well, so what?

Well, the real fact is that Bobby Sumler is a big man—at 5-foot 7-inches. But he plays like a seven footer. More important, he operates on the floor as if he was the only "chosen" star in the sky. Flashing here. Sparkling there.

**WILL HE BE LUCKY?**
We, in Monday Nite Football, know all too well the odds are against Bobby progressing beyond the college ranks. That would be the case even if he weren't built close to the ground. Even so, it is refreshing to see a Calvin Murphy or a Spud Webb "in the making." He'll need lots of work. And, in case good luck does pass his way, we'll have the pleasure of knowing it all started down at Kemper.

# Monday Nite Footballers
**Commentary**
**By Jim Nunnelly**

***

# 39th and Agnes: 'The Clyde Kemp Trail'

"Do not follow where the path may lead. Go instead where there is no path and leave a trail."

That quote seems to characterize Clyde Kemp. What's so special about Clyde Kemp? Is it that he worked nights at the Post Office? No. Or is it he's a regional loan specialist for HUD? No. Or is it that he's part of a family reunion that gives a scholarship to a fellow family member? No. Well, what's the big deal about this trail he leaves?

Simple.

Clyde just happens to be the winningest coach ever, in junior high Catholic League circles. And, though not recognized for his endless efforts, he's done this for twenty years—with little fanfare, just an uncontrollable obsession to serve. And he loves this coaching position and he shows you how much by the many stories he tells; like the one about an eighth grader who wanted to get married. Or about the fact that he's lost less than 50 games in his whole career. Or about the fact that he coached one "ghetto Ca-

ball with the Montreal Expos. On and on, like a proud history teacher who's just been honored. And teacher he is, let's look in.

For Clyde, entering the basketball coaching field was a complete accident. Not knowing a thing about basketball, he consented to sit with his son's team. This was way back in 1967, at little Blessed Sacrament School (now St. Martins) at 39th and Agnes. He was just to sit on the bench, not coach. Just act like a chaperone for a bunch of Catholic school kids who needed an adult to play. He started coaching that night and the rest is Clyde's history.

Over four hundred wins later, Clyde has convinced his whole family to get involved—his vivacious wife, Frankie, and his two sons. They all help and they're involved in the whole black Catholic movement, reinvesting themselves year after year.

But, Clyde's a Baptist convert. You'd never know it because his respect in the Catholic community is so profound and far reach-

tholic" now playing base-

**Footballers**
Continued from page 14
ing.

His confident influence ranges from spearheading the rules of the Catholic Sports Program to guiding the merger of inner city Catholic schools. He’s a rock and everyone around him knows it and they all expect him to be “solid and sound” in his judgments.

Especially, his players and the other 34 teams in the eighth grade Catholic League admire his coaching prowess. He just keeps winning championships year after year. Of course, he only tells you it’s the “team” concept or it’s the “fast break” that makes these junior high kids excel to impossible heights. But it’s really Clyde’s “dedication and drive” to make every young man a good student first and an athlete second. That’s it, that’s it, Clyde’s key to his success. He pushes the developing youngster “to the max” and therein lies the “secret map” of the trail.

And he gets those results, too. Ask George Word. Or Cebron Robinson. Or Ricardo Broils. Or Anthony Augmon. Or Al Newman of the Montreal Expos. They all studied the basketball teachings of Clyde the Great. And from there went on to play college ball.

Speaking of study? Where did Clyde learn about basketball? As an intrusion into his privacy, he wasn’t a basketball player in high school. And his speculative football playing days at Sumner in Kansas City, Kas., were cut short when his coach found his mother’s signature forged on a consent form. Further when reflecting back to his childhood days in Mississippi, he really dreamed of being a baseball player.

So, how did it start? He learned from training films bought at Seven-Up sponsored clinics. Yes, that was years and many lessons ago. Today, he still coaches the same eighth grade. And his Vince Lombardi-like style makes him trusted, feared and respected.

Things have changed a little today, since way back in the 60’s. Now his teams sport classy red and white uniforms. And he’s been appointed to the Diocesan School Board. His wife, Frankie, is the cheerleader coach. And his two sons, even the one responsible for him coaching the 5th grade, share his coaching duties at the St. Martin de Porres School (formerly Blessed Sacrament.)

As if divinely guided, he smiles and says about this total involvement in this volunteer sports program, “we (his family) just feel like we need to be there”. And kids, parents, school teachers, nuns, priests, bishops, coaches, all can find him every evening in the fall and winter, down at the Clyde Kemp trail.

Koren Wah, a seventh-grade student at Northeast junior high school, receives congratulations from IBM Executive Jerry Stabenow after being selected to throw out the first ball at a recent Royals game. Koren was one of 1,000 seventh graders honored by the Royals and IBM Corporation for their dedication to staying in school and getting a good education. # Monday Nite Footballers
**By Isaac Gardner Jr.**
**Guest Columnist**

Recently, Jim Nunnelly and I were privileged to enjoy a fun evening at Arrowhead with some of the Chiefs, their staff, and supporters. Given that Commissioner Nunnelly and I have opinions that are consistently at variance, I will catagorize the people I met, the activities, and present my opinion of the evening.

Head Coach, Frank Ganz—articulate, good P.R. skills, astute, political savvy, knows how to work a crowd. Time will tell whether he was sagacious in focusing on running backs and not giving the same attention to the offensive line which was exactly that, offensive.

Management—Les Miller and Don Steadman— positive, cordial, both seemed interested in establishing a positive relationship with the black community.

Former players Otis Taylor and Buck Buchannan— legends in their own time. My high regard for both men has escalated as I have observed them decades later in the real world.

Current player Stephonne Paige—super athlete, candid, honest, realistic, intelligent. I was highly impressed with Stephonne, not because of his athletic ability, but because there are obvious indications that he will do equally well after he leaves my favorite sport.

Food and beverages— plentiful, palatable, having been conditioned by Gates Bar-B-Q, I find that his competitors have a hard act to follow.

Film Highlights of 1986— entertaining. I have been and still am a loyal supporter and ardent critic of the Chiefs. I enjoyed viewing some super plays by some super athletes. However, what the films didn’t show may have been more revealing than what was shown. Granted the specialty teams did an excellent job under the leadership of Frank Ganz.

Though I am not a supporter or fan of John Mackovic, I think it was callous and in poor taste not to mention the contributions of Mackovic and the rest of the coaching staff during the 1986 season.

The overall event—very good managerial and administrative effort. The Chiefs, like the Kansas City, Mo., School District, have had their difficulties but are now on the upswing.

# Chatter, Commentary and Conversation

Nostalgia... that's when you want to go back in time or you are homesick or you moan for the good times gone by.

Well, that's how the Monday Niters spent their evening, over at Ed West's posh, Raytown-Kansas City home. Though the surroundings were colorful, we all found ourselves drifting back in time. . . you know, the good old days. And the liveliest journey back-in-time was remembering old movies. Here are some of the favorites. . . Imitation of Life. . . To Kill A Mockingbird. . . Gone With The Wind. . . Island In The Sun. . . Godfather. . . To Sir, With Love. . . Liberation of Lord Byron Jones. . . Patch Of Blue. . . From Here To Eternity. . . and on and on. We literally forgot about the Dallas game and spent our time seeing who could recall the all-time great big screen productions. And further, these guys, well into their '60s and '70s, could recite with clarity. . . some of the most unforgettable lines, first stated in these classics. Nostalgia lives in us, big-time!

And finally we turned our attention to the Chiefs. Last week, the Chiefs held on dearly to beat San Diego, just by three. And we saw something there that we hadn't seen for a long time. . . relentless pursuit of winning. So many of the recent Chief teams just gave up and failed to give us "60 minutes of excellence." And then again, that nostalgia thing set in. All of us remember the Chiefs in their heyday.

Buck Buchanan, Willie Lanier, Jerrell Wilson, Otis Taylor, Mike Garrett and on and on. We remember the alacrity that each player displayed after every play. We remember that even in defeat, they held their heads high, not needing to explain anything. For they knew that their "absolute best" had been given. Nostalgia. . . homesickness. we want to go back to those times. . . oh, so badly.

Can ol' Herm take us there? That is the question. And we don't know the answer. Fans still are shaky when the Chiefs get ahead early in the game. There is this "little voice" inside us all, knowing full well that the lead could evaporate before the game ends. That would have never happened in the glory days. As "apostles of nostalgia", we remember that Ol' Warpaint (horse mascot) would keep running and running, up and down the sidelines, signaling another Stenerud field goal or another Otis Taylor touchdown pass. If you were born after 1970, you don't know what I'm talking about.

Sadly and full of nostalgia, we remember there was a time when the Chiefs were feared, respected, talented, and could beat anybody. They just kept on "matriculating down the field". Monday Niters want those days back. . . before we all die!

# Monday Nite Footballers
# Commentary
**By Jim Nunnelly**

# The Strike And The Big Numbers

Seventeen million. Two-hundred thirty thousand. Sixty-three thousand.

At the second meeting of the season, we, Monday Niters kept repeating those numbers. But, before we explain the underlying significance of those numbers, let's look closer at what we think about the strike. And then we'll return to numbers. When Gene Upshaw of the Players' union announced the strike Monday night during the halftime of the Jets-Patriots game, the endless opinions began, starting with a local dentist.

In his infinite and quiet wisdom, Dr. Charles Allen spoke for the fans. After thoughtful and careful consideration, he felt that we could look for a rise in ticket prices, if not now, then next year. Coach Jerry Price countered by stating that money was not the "real issue" in this strike. To the contrary, though Walt Livingston, an elementary educator, said, "The owners just don't want to give up the money."

And the strike chatter prevailed. Here and there. And on and on. Until Carl Clark, an inspired union man, spoke with renewed enthusiasm in favor of the strike. He asserted that the rest of us did not understand union issues and indicated a strong need for unrelenting unity among players. And, then the clatter turned to a vote.

And when those favoring the strike were counted, there was a slight majority, favoring the players. But, shortly after this, another dimension arose. Another side. An unexplored position—neither favoring the players not the owners. Led by Ike Gardner, a school district supervisor, some felt that the entire mess was strictly uncalled for. The owners' and the players are at fault for allowing this dreadful thing to occur, in the first place. And, though Ike is usually a maverick and an apostle of obstinance, this time he hit the nail on the head. Nothing is good about this strike. Consequently, no one should feel sorry for either group—neither the owners or the players. Obviously, the owners come from a plantation mindset. They own; therefore they decide and control. And the players, with a less understandable position, come from an I-have-been-taken-advantage-of kind of mindset. Both sides need to take a lesson in public relations. Or better still, a semester-long course in Consumer Affairs, because neither side has won the public's support. To go even further, neither group will ever be seen standing in the food stamp line. For, they all make aplenty.

Over and above the money they make from ticket sales, the owners get an average $17 million from television. And, the players average income is listed at a handsome sum of $230,000. In fact, one Monday Niter reported that John Elway, rifle-armed quarterback of the Denver Broncos, makes $63,000 a game. That's just not something one should strike about.

But, as history has repeatedly shown, both sides will squabble and squander, until both sides get more. More of this or that. But, more. And, we fans will have to pay more. Or spend more. Or wait more. Moreover, wouldn't it be delightful that when both sides agree, the fans would go on strike. Let them play to an empty house. Now, that's big numbers.

# Monday Nite Footballers Commentary
# By Isaac Gardner-Guest Columnist

## Perspective On The Hall Of Fame

For almost a decade I have been trying to mo tivate the Monday Night Footballers to take a trip to Canton, Ohio and par ticipate in the Hall of Fame activities.

This past week, a few of us decided to make the sojourn and found it to be 'quite enlightening.' We were particularly proud that one of our own Kansas City Chiefs, Len Dawson, was inducted into the Football Hall of Fame. It seems that the local television media shared our pride. However, I am equally, if not more, proud to see two other Hall of Famers sharing the stage with Len Dawson. They were two of the premier linebackers of all time, Willie Lanier and Bobby Bell. It should be pointed out that both Willie Lanier and Bobby Bell had shorter playing careers than Len Dawson, but both made the Hall of Fame before 'Cool Len ny.'

This does not detract from the achievements of Dawson, but it clearly sig nifies the stellar perfor mances of two super ath letes. I say all of the above to pose the following query. Why didn't the local tele vision media give the two super black athletes the same coverage they gave a good white athlete?

Since the answer is ob vious, I'll waste no more time dealing with the mo tives of our 'fair-minded' television executives.

The football game itself was at best lackluster.

If the quality of play against the Forty-Niners was an omen of things to come, we are in for a very long season.

The most disappointing aspect of the game was that most of the points were given up by the specialty teams. Last year, our spe cialty team frequently made the difference be tween victory and defeat. In that, our head coach got his recognition on the performance of our spe cialty teams, one would think that our specialty teams would be ready even if the offense and defense were not ready.

People are already ask ing if the appointment of Ganz is a classical example of the "Peter Principle."

If there was a bright spot in the game, it has to be Christian Okoye. Most of his yardage was gained against rookies, so 1 still maintain my position of wait and see on the young man from Nigeria.

The trip was informative, entertaining, and very worthwhile.

Chiefs may have a decent team this year.

The game was so "Ho Hum" that the entire group spent most of the second half in the Hall of Fame viewing the exhibits and films. Other than an of fensive line, a good quar terback, a tight end, three good linebackers, and prov en running backs, the

# Monday Nite Footballers
# Commentary
**By Jim Nunnelly**

# Okoye...Is Okay...

The greatest thing in this world is not where he is but in what direction he is heading. The "he" is Christian Okoye and he's heading for the Arrowhead Stadium. On Tuesday, the Chiefs selected him as their second-round draft choice. Let's look this newcomer over—his career, his life, and his strengths and weaknesses, and his direction.

First, his college career. By now, everyone knows that he was an All-American from a little-known college called Azusa Pacific. And unless one is completely blind, one can tell he's big, weighing a giant-size 253 pounds and standing 6'1". On top of all that, he's fast, clocked at 4.4 in the 40 yard dash. Go on. Go on.

And just think he didn't even play football, prior to three years ago. That's because he came to the United States on a track and field scholarship. His specialty—a worldclass discus thrower. From Nigeria. The remainder of his family, a father and three sisters, are still in the homeland.

Now, to the football field. Even though he has never played a down on artificial turf, he scored 21 touchdowns on natural grass. But, he doesn't consider that a big problem—at least, not an unsolvable problem. And in his final two years, he gained over 3,000 yards rushing.

Okay, Okoye, okay.
And he's really serious about doing well, owing to his older-than-usual twenty five years. So what makes Ike Gardner (fresh from the marriage altar) say, "let's wait and see" when asked about Christian Okoye?

Is it what the critics say?
They say he fumbles a lot. And they go on to caution fans that he runs "straight up," thereby making Okoye unreliable and injury-prone. Okoye answers his critics by simply chalking it up to his first years of football. However, he quickly points out that that was a "yesterday" problem and all is well, now.

And from the way Kansas City fans have been singing his praises and raising their 1987-season hopes, they believe that he has corrected those rookie jitters, too.

Jerry Price is one of those. He's convinced that Okoye is the "sleeper" of the entire NFL draft. Jerry expects Okoye to rise to the status of a Gale Sayers or a Mike Garrett, both former Chiefs' draft choices. In fact, most are thinking that he will be better than first-round pick, Paul Palmer of Temple. *So what's the bottom line.*

The bottom line is this. Okoye is okay.
He has size, speed, a proven career, intelligence, and modesty. And we, Monday Nites, like the fact that he doesn't murder the English language, when interviewed. After all, he's one of the few athletes that will have earned a college degree—before entering pro football. What else can we say, except that we wish him the best that Kansas City can offer. And, like Avis, he'll try harder. And that trait alone makes him all right with us. As we said earlier, he's heading in the right direction. Or again, Okoye is okay.

# Monday Nite Footballers
**Commentary.**
**By Jim Nunnelly**

# Tracy Cherry: Moving Up and Giving Back...Uniquely

"Moving up and giving back." That was the theme for last week's Women's Day program at St. James Methodist church. More personally, the theme applies neatly to a brilliant, unusual, athletic, and unique lady, Tracy Cherry. Let's see how.

First of all, Tracy was a scholarly athlete at Ursinus college, a small private Jesuit school, just outside Philadelphia. There she earned a 3.5 grade point average, majoring in Physical Education, with a minor in Business and Psychology. As if that was not enough, she was awarded All-America honors in field hockey. Now, how's that for being unique, a black female All America on the Dean's List. Moving up. Let's go on.

After graduation from this well respected institution, she was flown to Kansas City—to interview for and accept a teaching position at a local school, Barstow. There she became the first (and only) black faculty member in the entire history of the well-known school. Besides teaching Physical Education, she was appointed Coach of the Girls' field hockey team. Under her tutelage, the team overtook archrival Pembroke, in September 1982. Moving up, farther. What about this field hockey?

Simply put, field hockey is her "pride and joy." But her touchstone and strength is centered around her family. Being the only girl (again unique) with a cast of four brothers has entitled Tracy to a special position with her father. It was with his encouragement and guidance that she was able to select her college early, in the seventh grade. He wanted her in the best school, academically and athletically. Ursinus offered both. And her love for field hockey was sustained. Thanks, Dad for helping me move up.

But, when she talks about her mother, a heavy and deep-seated, emotional high settles on her face. That's because she's really close to her mother. Not only because of her "only girl" status, but because she respects her mother's undying devotion to her—and her four brothers.

Her mother made sure that all of them went to college, in spite of the fact that her parents were divorced when Tracy was twelve. Her mother helped Tracy "move up." Let's get back to Tracy's real love.

And, again she smiles widely and sincerely when she resumes talking about Field Hockey. She emphasizes that the sport is unique and requires a set of unique characteristics...strong legs, rangy wrist work, and the finesse and grace of an Olympic figure skater. And Tracy possesses and wears these attributes—in a pleasant and charming way. Enough of moving up, let's see what and how she gives back.

Not only does she sing in the choir at Mount Vernon Baptist church, she also teaches aerobics at the Linwood YMCA. And besides attending all her brother's football games (Deron Cherry of the Kansas City Chiefs), she finds enough time to talk to her mother every day, long distance from Palmyra, New Jersey. Talk about giving back. And the list could go on.

Finally, anyone can see that here is somebody who knows how to be somebody. Of all her honors, on and off the field, she believes deeply, in moving up and giving back—at the same time. Only a few people are ever treated to a set of unique experiences, academically and athletically. But, Tracy is a unique person, and she wears it well.

# Monday Nite Footballers Commentary
By James T. Nunnelly

# Ed West: A Lesson In Little Extras

A native of Osawatomie, Kas. Graduate from Pittsburg State. Married, three children. Taught basic electricity, Lincoln high school in the early '60s. Likes to fish in the Kansas reservoirs and the Canadian clear lakes. Enjoys tinkering with all kinds of electronic devises. An avid sports enthusiast. All of these phrases describe Ed; but none better than the last one—an avid sports enthusiast. Let's see how.

First, Ed clearly remembers two championship games in his lifetime — one in high school and the other in college. One in which his team lost, the other in which his team won. And, he put together a valuable lesson and philosophy from both.

Like many athletes, Ed remembers vividly the day his Osawatomie high school team lost to Rosedale of Kansas City, Kas. That was

way back in 1957. And his memory is still impeccable. And a sound of disappointed excitement still permeates his voice. That day Ed, his four other black teammates, and the rest of his team went that “little extra.”

But, they lost.

And Ed still talks about it with a yesterday's freshness. Rosedale, a big school, beat Osawatomie, a little school. By one point. One lousy point, but, a big point. And Osawatomie had beat other big schools. But, that day David could not slay Goliath. From that, Ed learned that even you can learn from a loss. Especially, when you give your all and all; and even with that little extra, you can still lose. But, a real champion never gives up.

And he didn't give up. Two years later, as a sophomore corner back at Pittsburg State, he found himself in the first NAIA

championship game—Pittsburg State of Kansas verses Hilldale of Michigan. That day he was a star, especially on defense.

He intercepted two passes and returned a punt 60 yards, setting up a key touchdown. Pittsburg was proud that day, winning 26-21. And Ed was too. He and his team gave a little extra. And you can hear him smile about that game any time he talks about it. It was like a lesson. For extra credit in life.

Speaking of life, Ed holds a creative and steady interest in his work—as production supervisor at AT&T. Besides being athletic, he’s alert, ambitious, attentive and articulate. He has to be right; because his department produces 30,000 electronic circuit breakers daily. And every single day, he experiences the agonies of defeat and the thrills of victory, just

like in those championship games.

But, he gives that “little extra.” For he knows that his company and his superiors are concerned that foreign competitors will force them out of the marketplace. And the American companies must ban together. He’s proud to be a part of this happening. You can detect that challenge, deeply rooted in his smile. Just like in those championship games.

And, that little extra emerges, again. It’s evident that he’s learned well from his early athletic experiences. And, maybe that’s what sports teaches us, that the difference between ordinary and extraordinary is that “little extra.” And, that you must apply that “little extra” to everything you do, especially your chosen field.

Just like Ed does.

# Monday Nite Footballers
Commentary
By Jim Nunnelly

# Choice, Not Chance, Determines Destiny

San Antonio, Philadel- phia, New York, Portland, L.A. Clippers, and Atlanta had something in common on draft day—Monday June 22. They all wisely drafted seven footers in the 1987 talent-rich, NBA draft. Even the first team to pick, San Antonio, drafted David Robinson, a seven-foot, three incher out of the Na- vy.

But, one team used their first round draft pick to take a 5 foot 3 inch guard from Wake Forest. Yes — not seven foot, not six foot, by a five foot, three inch midget. What would compel the Washington Bullets to waste a precious, first round choice to pick a very short basketball player? Before we comment, let’s look in on the player and see if we can find some reason for this madness.

**Bugsy's Background**
First of all, the honoree is a “little ant” named “Bugsy” (real name Ty- rone Bogues). Besides be- ing the shortest ever to be drafted into the giant- dominated NBA, he was once the teammate of Reg- gie Williams, 6'7" first round choice for the Los Angeles Clippers.

Okay, that was at the Dunbar high school in Bal- timore.

And recently, his claim to fame was his outstanding showmanship at the World Championship games in Spain. There he dazzled the international fans with his fancy footwork and pre- cision-like, bullet passing.

Okay, so what do the Mon- day Niters think?

**The Fellows**
**Are Reserved**

Well, the guys all have expressed “guarded” res- ervation about this “gutsy” move on the part of the Bullets. Both Paul Booker and Ike Gardner think that it's just a publicity stunt and no more. Already, Washington has the tallest player at seven foot eight. And, with “Bugsy” Bogues, they would have the tallest and the shortest players.

Again, so what?

Ike further quips, that to be successful, Washing- ton will have to play Atlanta every week, suggestng that the only player that Tyrone can adequately guard is the Hawks Spud Webb (he’s 5'7"). Ed West says that he'll have to be able to jump through the moon. And, who knows, maybe he can. However, publicity or jumping ability do not win games.

**Commentary**

To win an appropriate share of one hundred and sixty two games, a team should choose the best play- ers possible. Thus, every game and everybody counts. Can you imagine little Bugsy trying to guard 6'9" Magic Johnson? How silly, point guard or not, it's just plain foolish. And, it tends to make a mockery out of this serious game of professional basketball.

But, even more seriously, we advise Washington to use choice, not chances, to determine their destiny. Put the best, most-likely- to-succeed players on the floor. Competition among the pro teams is fierce.

# Monday Nite Footballers Commentary
By Jim Nunnely

**Bo Jackson: Money Maker And Mind Changer**

Vincent Edward Jack-
son. Bessemer, Ala., Bo.
Auburn university. 1985
Heisman Trophy winner.
Three sport letterman at
Auburn, football, baseball,
and track. Drafted by the
Tampa Bay football Buc-
caneers. Signed with the
Kansas City Royals, in-
stead.

Said he was going to play
forever for the Royals. And
he signed a football "hob-
by" contract Tuesday, July
14, with the Los Angeles
Raiders instead.

And that's when the stuff
hit the fan.

Amid all the opinions on
whether Bo Jackson is trust-
worthy or not, it does sell
newspapers. And it does
capture the attention of
all sport fans, everywhere.
Most important, there was

a clever escape clause, in
his contract. That clause
allowed him to change his
mind and play football in-
stead.

And "Ole Bo" has had
a history of changing his
mind about one thing or
another.

So what's the beef?
What's the harm? Let's
explore.

Simply, the beef is that
the players and fans feel
ostracized and betrayed.
Bold Bo announced his in-
tentions to pursue a "hobby
in the NFL." And, they
holler as if the world of
baseball is not business-
like and brusque. And most
Kansas Citions were both
confused, surprised, and
touched by his decision.
But, we Monday Niters sup-
port Bo's mind-changing,

money-making tactics.
Ike Gardner curtly says,
"He ought to earn all he
can, while he can." Paul
Booker follows by stating
that Bo is only exercising
his contract. Paul further
states that others would
like to do as Bo has done.

But, none of us really
know what was or is on
Bo's mind. We all know
that here's a guy who is
equally good at two major,
high-paying, sports. Some
submit that Bo is just going
home to play his first and
only love, football. For base-
ball lovers, he certainly
has achieved a fair enough
record. In his second season
as a Royal, he has hit 18
homers and drove in a team-
leading 45 runs. That's com-
mendable for a sophomore.

Well, no matter what's
really on his mind, we know
he had made a lot of
moves-both on and off the
field. He had been blessed.

and enabled to choose one,
or the other, or both sports.
Cleverly and legally, he
got what he wanted, when
he wanted it, like he wanted
it. And, we have to support
Ole' Bo, for covering, and
then stealing all the bases.

Ollie Hubbard said it
best, when he said, "Let
Bo run." This time for the
Los Angeles Raiders. Who
knows, maybe in 1988, he'll
run track-instead.

# Monday Nite Footballers Commentary
By Jim Nunnely

| **Big Jim Irsay:** | **On The Wanting Edge** |
| :--- | :--- |
| Everything depends on what we are capable of wanting. Whoever said that must have been thinking of the young general manager of the Indianapolis Colts. Before we demon- strate how that statement applies, let's take a look at this year's edition of the Colts. | $10,000, for the weight room. SMU never had any- thing like that. |
| Last year, the Colts won just three games and lost 13. The team is now 4-6 and in first place in the NFC East. We have to ex- plain just what motivates a team to become the NFL's turnaround dream team? The easiest answer is coach- ing. | Speaking of SMU, Eric Dickerson, the Colts's la- test acquisition, is also an All-American from that school. Eric is considered by many to be the game's best running back. And, we all know the contribu- tion he has made to football, and more recently to the playoff-bound Colts. Ev- erytime he runs for a first down with that tornado- like glide, the Indiana fans are reminded that the state is now famous for more than its college basketball team, the Indiana Hoosiers. |
| That's because it's no secret that the Colts were right down there with Jim. on Bay. Surely, they needed something like a coach, for they lost their first thir- teen games last year. And then Ron Meyer was ap- pointed head coach and the 1986 Colts won their last three games. Meyer is a product of the Texas- based school, Southern Methodist University. | **COMMENTARY** |
| For the record, SMU is the alma mater of Jim Ir- say, the Colt's general man- ager. Young Irsay was a weightlifter there. Though there is no obvious reason, the SMU name keeps popping up. Is it that a school like SMU has some- thing else that sparks this upward lift? | Though it may be easy to conclude that there is something magic about SMU. No. No. But, the mag- ic does lie in the courage, competence, and confi- dence of the Colts's 28-year- old general manager. He hired Meyer from a school that had been under in- vestigation. And he went on to acquire Eric Dick- erson, who was full of prob- lems and demanding more pay. The young general manager went for it. He |
| Paul Booker says it's money. Paul believes the owner, Robert Irsay, will spend millions to produce a winner at Indianapolis. And Paul, in his unassum- ing way, may be right. For the elder Irsay is willing to give an player $50,000 for a year-round weight- lifting program. And, he permitted his weightlifting son to purchase a stereo sound system costing a cool | doubled Eric's pay and the payoff is in the making. Big Jim Irsay continues to press on—not only in lifting weights but in lifting the horizons of a team and a city. He wanted "an |

edge." And the two SMU thoroughbreds, Meyer and Dickerson, gave him that edge. And the record shows that he's still getting it.

Again, everything de- pends on what we are ca- pable of wanting. Hats off to Jim Irsay.

# Monday Nite Footballers Commentary
**By Jim Nunnelly**

# Who Is Better: Bird or Magic?

Basketball fans every- where have compared “the Bird” with “the Magic.” And, if you listen to the daily Superfan show on radio, you’ll hear it repeatedly. Bird is better. Magic Johnson is better. And on and on. And the fact that their respective teams are again competing for the championship, just fuels the “war” even further.

Well, we Monday Niters usually don’t talk about basketball—it’s off season for us. But, because everyone else is rendering opinions so freely, it does deserve our commentary. We’d like to talk mainly about salary and floor ability. Then, after that we’ll add some insight to this controversy.

Now to the bucks.

**Both Are Paid Well**

L a r r y Bird makes $1,800,000 annually, on a seven year contract, from the Boston Celtics. His counterpart, Magic Johnson enjoys a lucrative pay- check from Los Angeles, making a cool million each year, for twenty-five years, regardless of how long he can play. So, Bird makes more sooner and Magic makes less longer. Let’s move onto the court.

**On The Court**

On the radio show, whites generally pick Bird and the blacks pick Magic. And, it’s been that way since they met in the now-resurrected Indiana State-Michigan State game of some years ago. Now, let’s ask the fellows what they think.

Ed West quickly points out that it’s a “tough comparison.” He says Magic is better, mainly because of his all around ability. He adds that Magic can win tough games. And fair-minded Carl Clark agrees. But, Carl thinks that the Bird is just as important, and plays just as well. Further, both players take control of the game—Bird from forward position and Magic from the guard. Even, Carl says. And what about scoring?

Jerry Price is kind of like Carl Clark, when he addresses the shooting issue. “Bird is a better perimeter (outside) shooter and Magic is the best inside shooter.” Obviously the two have demonstrated countless times how true this statement is. And all of us know that Bird won the Three-Point Competition outright. Decided yet? Let’s wrap up.

**Commentary**

Tough comparison. It certainly is. But, it appears Magic gets the nod. Not only for his personal contributions, but for his “knack” for making his fellow Lakers rise to greater heights of excellence. He’s smart, courtwise, and he knows where everybody is, all the time. That is rare talent, anywhere in the NBA. And year after year, he is among the leaders in assists. Face it, he’s a bonafide floor general, without peer. A true leader. And on top of all that, he is a “picture of excellence”—unknown to and unbelieved by many. He is truly magic.

# Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim "Grand Dad" Nunnelly Predicting Since 1970

## Kansas City Chiefs vs. Houston Texans
12:00 p.m. KCTV 5

| **Charles Allen** | **Paul Booker** | **Carl Clark** | **Bubba Cleaver** | **Ike Gardner** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| KC by 14 Must win to begin! | KC by 10 Can't stop the run! | Houston by 7 No pre-season! | KC by 10 AIR-time! | Houston by 7 Still in preseason! |
| **Lloyd Greenfield** | **Ollie Hubbard** | **Jim Nunnelly** | **Ed West** | |
| KC by 3 With much luck! | Houston by 12 Lane College Alum! | KC by 3 All about Johnson! | KC by 10 LJ is back! | |

# Chatter, Conversation, and Commentary

Ever watch "House" on television? It's a reality show, where the title character is slightly impaired and occasionally is seen as human i.e. he may not be the know-it-all that doctors seem exude.

But, it is an excellent show because through it all, humanity triumphs. Well, that also explains the plight of our Chiefs. Through it all, they manage to somehow triumph. Last year, they actually made the back-door-playoffs.

They stumbled and fumbled but somehow they made it to the first round. . . but were immediately eliminated. This year, this same reality is going to set in. Just one glance at the schedule confirms this assertion. The Chiefs have to play both Super Bowl teams. . . Indianapolis and Chicago. Then, the Chiefs will have to play San Diego twice. That's four losses, already. Add in a split with Denver and an improved Oakland. Figure in five blown games, where the Chiefs fail to tackle; then throw a series of unexplainable interceptions; and then, cop penalties that no one can fathom. To top it off, Cincinnati and Tennessee will just flat out kick the Chiefs' butts. That means the Chiefs will only win five games. But, Houston is one of them. Dr. House, can you heal thyself?

**Miami, Florida**

**5:25 p.m. KCTV 5**

| Charles Allen | Paul Booker | Carl Clark | Bubba Cleaver | Ike Gardner | Lloyd Greenfield |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dungy by 7 That’s the way it is! | Dungy by 10 He’s the oldest! | Dungy by 10 I’ve always been a Colt! | Dungy by 12 Too much Dungy, Period. | Dungy by 7 Kansas City Connection! | Dungy’s awesome passing game! |

| Ollie Hubbard | Jim Nunnelly | Ed West |
|---|---|---|
| Dungy by 8 Been at it longer! | Smith by 3 Smith, underdog wins this one! | Dungy by 7 Experience wins out! |

| Donna Stewart (Publisher) | Tracy Allen (Sports Writer) | Eric Wesson (Sports Guy) | George Dunmore (By George) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Indianapolis by 10 Colts are simply the better team | Indianapolis by 7 Peyton takes charge | Indianapolis by 17 Too much offense for Chicago | Indianapolis by 3 Better quarterback, better kicker |

***

# Urban American Outdoors

| Candice Price | Indianapolis by 14 | Tony Dungy’s time |
|---|---|---|
| Wayne Hubbard | Indianapolis by 7 | It’s Peyton Manning’s year! |

***

# Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

Mary Lynn Clark said it best. This has been a relatively quiet Super Bowl. Not much said about “all” of it, because these two black coaches have broken through the “glass ceiling”.

Or is it because there just isn’t much to talk about? Well, let’s see if we can raise the “talk” bar. As you have noted, we predicted the “coaches” this week rather than the teams. That’s because this is moment in history where everyone wins. Either way, a black coach wins Super Bowl XLI. OK? And America wins. Because we can proudly say that a black coach has won and coached in a Super Bowl. And, last, Caucasian announcers will find it a bit awkward for either over-emphasizing or under-stating it. It will be interesting to see how they handle it.

But, back to Mary Lynn’s comment about the “quietness”. Now, from where I sit, every African American is talking about it, proudly. But the television sponsors are not. They ought to be talking about this like they talked about Elway, finally making it to his first Super Bowl. The way it appears, is that Manning will replace Big Bad John as the hype center piece. And that is an affront to all of us black people. This two-team black coaching thing is right up there with Jackie Robinson, breaking into base ball way back in the ’40s. And it should be on every media outlet in the country. Foreign countries ought to know that America has made good on some of its promises (not all of them, just some).

But, reality tells me that the giant media outlets will treat this rather “low-key”. Thus, I predict that very little media will be said about all of this on Sunday and the days leading up to it. And quite frankly, I mad about it. Whatever you do, don’t say that it doesn’t matter. Sunday may be the last time, for a long time, when we all can sing “We Shall Overcome” with meaning and purpose. After all, to me and to you, Super Bowl Sunday will be a sacred part of Black History Month (lest we forget).

Let all rejoice and be glad in it. Thanks, Mary Lynn for nudging us to speak up.

# Conversation, Chatter and Commentary

Once again K.C. is at a fever pitch with visions of the Super Bowl “dancing in its head.”
Before you go off on the deep end, note well, in 1995 a team far less formidable than Denver came to town and left us crying with our 13-3 record.
My objective sense tells me that we will be crying again.
Emotionally, I will be in my seat behind the Chiefs bench cheering for the home team.
For those of you who will be crying that river, I strongly recommend that you go to any store other than Dillard’s to purchase your towels.
The Monday Nite Niters wish the entire community a felicitous New Year.

# Monday Nite Footballers Predictions
By Jim Nunnelly

## 1998 Pre-Season Predictions
## For The K.C. Chiefs
## Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

Yes, it's that time of the year. It's time to focus on the Chiefs and their upcoming search. Will the Chiefs go all the way to the Super Bowl this year, after promising for the last eight years? Don't forget the Chiefs won the AFC West Championship in 1997 and it was Denver who was the Wild Card entry. However, everyone knows that Denver beat the Chiefs 14-10 in the playoffs and went on to become World Champions, beating Green Bay in the Super Bowl.

But this is a new day and a new season. Nationwide, everyone says it will be the Chiefs and Packers in the Super Bowl. However, skeptical fans here in Kansas City still believe the Chiefs are "right" in the regular season...but real poor in the playoffs. The saying mostly is, "Always the bridesmaid and never the bride."
